Day 71: How to Change the World Part 1

September 03, 2025

I know. I know. It's been a while since my last post. Apologies for those who may have been waiting for a new post. It hasn't been easy moving from one place to the other, leaving an old job for a new job, and so on.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
But I am back! Although I can't promise (not even to myself) that I would be updating my blog as frequently as I'may want, I will try my best to keep up with the requests on the topics and ideas about which you would like to see my writing. More than anything else, it's embarrassing that I have not been able to complete the "100 days of writing" mission in a year as planned. Well, that's life. One thing that I know for sure is my own writing style and pace: It's difficult for me to stick to a weekly schedule (although I will try to make it a habit) but whenever I have good ideas to write something about I could go 6-7 hours in a roll just writing one post after another.

So, do bare with me. This morning, I got up with some thoughts about what make me who I am. It could have a been a result of a difficult conversation I had with someone about whom I cared so dearly. That thought, though begun with how a seemingly caring conversation could go so wrong, had eventually led me to my thinking about what could I have done to change the situation. About a decade ago, I have set the goal in life to not chase after money or fame, but after things that would give me the total feeling of contentment with my life.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
I had come to the realization that I could live a good anonymous life with subsistent-leveled income (seriously, I have tried it). The possession of too much money and fame violates both the principles of minimalism and nonism (about which I had discussed also in this blog). One of the way to feel content with my life, to me, is to thinking constantly about a social engineering program that would make everyone turn inward to introspect the need of their inner self, rather than what they "think they need" as a result of the society's telling them what to do through peer pressure. I have, then, come to realize that two of the root causes of the problems we have in the world today are miscommunication and illogical thinking. These two factors constitute about 50% each for all the problems we have in the world today.

My argument is simple: we could live in a much better world if we can solve these two problems. I am determined to dedicate the rest of my life of solving these two problems. This blog post, however, is dedicated to discussing the first problem only. I promise that I'll write more in my next post about the power of logic as the way to combat logical thinking. Why do people fail to communicate?
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: As many philosophers, linguists, and thinkers have reminded us, our thought process is often both intertwined and non-linear. The problem we have with the verbal language is, then, that it forces us to communicate our thoughts through the non-intertwined and linear system. In other words, language always simplifies our thoughts. And it's also true that language constraints thoughts (i.e., what linguists call Sapir-Whorf Hypothesis ): We cannot possibly conceive any complicated matters without having the language as a system to connect the strings of words to make sense of our own thoughts, as a lens through which one could see the layout of our own idea, and finally as a tool by which one can construct a meaningful thought process. For example, when we think of anything that we may want, the essential pronoun "I" appears in your thought process, e.g., "I want that," and "I need that." The verbs that follow -- want and need -- are essential elements in the process of putting words in a string so that you could communicate your idea, first to yourself, and then to others by demanding or requesting that they'd provide you with what you "need" or "want."In other words, we need the "I" to be there as a conscious marker of our desire to construct a meaning that underpins our egotistical and, even, our biological needs.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
Most of the time, the subject + verb construction is followed either by an object or a conjunction; for instance, "I want water because I am thirsty." Can you give me an answer of why you're reading this blog post right now without thinking in terms of language? Can you answer me this question without using any nouns, pronouns, verbs, and adjectives? We think with words. We need words to think. Your brain is always going and it's estimated that we say between 300 - 1,000 words to ourselves per minute.

That is, we need language to think, and the limitation of our vocabulary is also the limitation on how effective we are in putting our thoughts into comprehensible strings of words. The trouble with language is no picnic. You may have witnessed people who we're talking about the same things but could not come to a conclusion that they did. You may have seen people who loved each other to death but their relationship ceases to continue to exist because they we're not able to communicate their love in the way acceptable to the other person. A classic case: You may have heard how people get upset about how they think the other person has a view on things that we're "too simplistic." True, it's may often the case that "that'someone" may have a simplistic view on things.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
But most of the time, I believe, it's not just that. To me, nothing is that'simplistic. Even when we want it to be so, unfortunately, what constitutes our "view on things" are our experience, upbringing, education, and so on. So, even on things about which we feel absolutely sure, they are often, if not always, an exception. Why is that?

-- Because our thoughts are way more complicated than the ability of languages to communicate them (well, some languages may do a better job in conveying the subtlety of the ideas, but that's a topic for another post). First, the language we know doesn't do justice to our thoughts. Second, we think that'something sounds "simplistic to us" owing to the fact that our brains, for the same reason as in why we couldn't put complicated thoughts into words, are often hostile toward strings of words that are too complicated. So, we tend to be more receptive, rather, to simple prose. But we are also hostile to simplicity because we are living in the world in which simplicity as such could induce misunderstanding, violence, racism, and other forms of discrimination; hence, we human beings are often caught in the middle: While we may want to communicate our true ideas, we are also not very receptive to complicated strings of words.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: The result is that we don't get the full meaning of other's ideas because the corporeal limit to how much a non-intertwined and linear expression of the strings of words could make you absorb all the meanings. A classic case: You may have heard how people get upset about how they think the other person has a view on things that we're "too simplistic." True, it's may often the case that "that'someone" may have a simplistic view on things. But most of the time, I believe, it's not just that. To me, nothing is that'simplistic. Even when we want it to be so, unfortunately, what constitutes our "view on things" are our experience, upbringing, education, and so on.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: So, even on things about which we feel absolutely sure, they are often, if not always, an exception, as in "I hate XXX but I would accept it if there's YYY involved." What's the meaning of "hate" (a dictionary definition: feel intense or passionate dislike for someone or something) then if an exception can be hate? I used to think that many people have simplistic ideas because they're incapable of seeing all dimensions of the problems or situations. But most of the time, as I have been taught in my training as a linguistic anthropologist, we tend to think that'something sounds simplistic simply because the message that we'receive is linear and non-intertwined, hence leaving out many of the important messages. The absence of those messages that did not make the cut is what make us feel as though such message is not as comprehensible as it'should or could be. So, as an anthropologist, I have learned to overcome this particular prejudice against something that may sound, when I first hear them, simplistic.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: Over time, I have become much more sensitive to those "messages that did not make the cut." What are the core ideas of those messages that have been intentionally left out owing to the inability of the language to convey all of them and still stay meaningful and understandable? Our brains are hostile toward something that is too complicated and tend to be more receptive, rather, to simplicity. But we wouldn't need language if we are only to communicate simplicity. We need language because we need to communicate with other human beings more than just the information regarding our biological needs. We think something is simplistic because of language, by nature, suppresses the full thought to convey.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
The linear and non-intertwined nature expressed through a string of words that we utter to communicate ideas often fails to convey our comprehensive ideas to others. People usually either hear what they want to hear (because our brains love to take the meaning simplistically) or do not have the patience for complicated thoughts carried through the lumping together of a complicated set of strings of words put together. What we have left here is miscommunication. In the perfect world, each of us would take, as the most important goal in life, the learning of how to communicate with others with precision, brevity, and respect, holding to heart the sincere goal of benefitting everyone engaging in the conversation. In the perfect world, intentional ridiculing of others and the failure to listen would be considered impolite and outlawed.

In the perfect world, we would have all the time and patience to pay attention to each other. Unfortunately, we are not living in that world. We're living in the world full of strangers (especially those living in cities). We're living in the world in which we deal with different sets of interest. We're living in the world that urges us to protect our own right to be individual and egotistical rather than give other people the benefit of the doubt.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: The problem gets worse when we also deal with translation from the one language to the other. You get the point. This was the whole premise of my writing about the Herzfeld's Rules (both Part 1 and Part 2 are available on this blog). I believe that changing the world isn't that difficult -- as we can eliminate almost 50% of the problems we have in the world today by our becoming better communicators who communicate to get the true points across and not to ridicule your counterpart or to show off that you happen to know what they do not know. Even if becoming a better communicator doesn't change the world, it would change your life.

Day 72: The Power of Logic (How to Change the World Part 2)

September 04, 2025

I have two stories to tell. All of which speak to one central theme: The power of logic is limitless. Let's begin. One: Hot Pot One afternoon I checked myself into a restaurant serving buffet lunch. It's a traditional-styled Chinese hot-pot.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
For those who have no idea what a hot pot is, it's a traditional Asian cooking method. They call it huoguo in China, shabu-shabu in Japan, and suki in Thailand. The differences between these are not that noticeable and mostly due to the ingredients particular to the certain geographical location. So, the one that I was eating was suki since it was in Bangkok. There's a pot of simmering broth in the middle of the table, and around which are plates of meat and vegetables.

All you have to do is to dip them into the broth and cook them. It's the best meal to be consumed with a lot of people: Because of the sharing nature of the meal, the more people is the merrier. There're many styles of hot-pot meals. My favorite is the Chongqing-styled, in which the broth consists mainly of Sichuan peppercorns and other mouth-numbing ingredients. It's the spiciest of all (and yes, spicy food is good for you according to Harvard T.H.

Chan School of Public Health ). It was on a weekday which might explain why there we'ren't so many people there; hence, I was receiving all the attention from the waiters and waitresses who would have to do the work in bringing whatever I was ordering to my table so that I could dump them into my pot to cook and then eat. "One more plate of squids, please," "More bok-choy (Chinese cabbage)," "Yes, I would like more broth, please," "Can I have more chili sauce?" "Two more plates of beef and lamp, please," "One more glass of Coke here," "A seafood platter followed by a bowl of udon (Japanese thick noodle)." That was when the logic kicked it. I stopped eating and started thinking. Cigarettes, of course, we'ren't permitted anywhere in the building but I was smoking them every day anyway because I was a chain smoker. Then I had a moment. I remember that I was sitting at the bottom frame of that'small window to the large and open empty field below.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: I was reading a self-help book and was inspired by every word of it. As I was looking the ashes falling down to the ground below, I had an epiphany: "What am I'doing?" It was that moment that I was able to come to terms with myself: I suddenly realized that in order to do those great things in the self-help book I was reading, I would first need a healthy body. "And why am I smoking this thing?" "It doesn't even feel good." That was the moment that the power of logic kicked in which helped me realized that the reasons I had been coughing every since I landed in Beijing might due to both my excessive smoking and the rising level of air pollution. "Yes, I have been coughing every day -- in the class and outside of the class -- I am feeling so disgusted by myself!" Finally, "I couldn't even breath properly because of the air pollution, why am I smoking this shit??" It's simply not logical for me to still be smoking. And that was it.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
I stopped smoking half way and threw that cigarette away -- together with the rest of the pack. In that moment, I'didn't think about the future (as in, I wouldn't have a healthy life 10 years from now I'd still be smoking then) or the past (as in, it's what killed my father). I was just thinking about the present: "Why am I'doing this illogical thing?" I never return to smoking ever again after that incident. That was the moment I realized that I'must have been a rational thinker. The power of will didn't help me quit'smoking (I tried and failed) but the power of reason and logic did.

It's not logical to want to live a good life and continue to smoke. That said, am I'missing smoking? Yes, I always have a dream about myself smoking with friends. text: I believe that it has to do with the way we human beings need to think for ourselves, in the present, about how we would like to live our life. Day 73: Six Basic Human Needs

September 05, 2025

While I was on the treadmill yesterday, I listened to Tony Robbins' podcast episode called "Six Basic Human Needs." For those who don't know who he is, Tony Robbins is one of the world's most famous inspirational speakers, business, and personal coaches, and gurus in self-improvement. He appeared in one of my favorite movies of all time Shallow Hal, featuring Jack Black and Gwyneth Paltrow. Robbins' Ted Talk "Why We Do What We Do" is number six in the top ten most watched and listened to Ted Talk of all time! [caption id="attachment_4472" align="alignleft" width="349"] Probably Robbins' most famous book. Noted: He looks much older now![/caption] His podcast had gotten me thinking about myself.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
Before we're going to that, let's talk about what's Robbins' "Six Basic Human Needs." Although he sometimes calls them interchangeably as "things," they're not really things (as in objects). He wasn't talking about food, houses, cars, or luxury watches. Sure, we need them (especially food) but they are varied across cultures. Robbins was talking about the "true needs as social beings." They are six "qualities" that fulfill the main purposes that we have to live our lives in the society. To Robbins, these six needs are universal.

He also extends his argument to say that the secret to understanding your business counterparts, partners, customers, and so on, is to understand the importance of these six basic needs in their lives.What are they?: Certainty, uncertainty (variety), significance and recognition, love and connection, growth, and contribution. Okay, after spending an hour listening to it, I felt that this was awesome. I felt that all of these qualifies make perfect sense to me. In addition to the fact that what brought Tony Robbins to come to this conclusion about these six needs was his 30-year-long research on human beings with whom he had interacted regularly in his line of work, he's also such an inspiring speaker. No wonder people pay a lot of money to his event!
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: Ok, back to his points. Let me try to summarize all of these points in a #nonphilosophy way. We all want a secure job and sometimes hostile to change because "certainty" is what underpins our sense of the continuity -- that our livelihood would continue into the future. That said, if everything is all about certainty, we would be bored to death; and that's why we would like some "uncertainties," challenges, surprises (that we want), and a wide variety of experience from time to time. There's an episode of Freakonomics Radio that I had also just listened to that talked about boredom as one of those things that everyone, even the unproductive people loathed.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: Yet, having job stability and variety in life isn't enough to most of us. We want to be different, to be unique, to have the "significance" in the society in which we live. This is the need that Robbins argue to underpin the reason why human beings often go out of our ways to search for money and fame, to the point that, as Dalai Lama has remarked, "Man surprised me most about humanity because he sacrifices his health in order to make money; then he sacrifices money to recuperate his health." But all in all, we want to be able to connect intimately with another human being. There are things that money and fame cannot always buy, and that is the unconditional love and connection with another human being(s). Beyond that, as a species, we need to develop ourselves in order to survive, and that's where the need for "growth" comes in, which eventually leads to how we think about the growth of another human being through our hard-wired ability to sympathize.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: We want to "contribute," because life is about creating meaning which does not often come from what you get but from what you give, including good news to share with your loved ones and things or money you give it away to those who need them. The best way to summarize this notion of contribution would be in the words of William Shakespeare himself: "The purpose of life is to discover your gift; the meaning of life is to give your gift away." You could discover your gifts by finding the certainty in your career, or by shaking things around by facing occasional uncertainties, or by finding what makes you special, or by finding the right person with whom you could connect, or by developing yourself -- but at the end of the day, if you don't find the way to give those gifts away, you'd be imbued with the sense of unfulfilling because there's a limit to all of the above qualities without your knowing that you life also means something to other people. So, those are Robbins' "Six Basic Human Needs" in a nutshell. So, here's what I really want to write about today, which is my take on these six basic human needs. I agree with Robbins, in principle, that the secret to any productive relationship is the reciprocity between the counterparts in understanding how each ranks these six qualities.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
You are not going to go well trying to befriend someone who unconditionally believes in challenges by giving him the sense of certainty and vice versa. Personally, I rank the following: Contribution; Love and connection; Growth; and, Uncertainty. As for the other two qualities - certainty and significance - I'don't find them to be important in the way I live my life. Let's go through them one by one. 1) Certainty.

I think the only thing that is certain about certainty is the fact that we are made to believe that which can be certain. I believe that Jean-Jacque Rousseau was right when he made an argument a few centuries ago that the sense of certainty, whether through job security, marriage, or commitment to a certain system of belief, is what the upper-class elites has created to keep the lower-class workers in check. By doing so, the upper-class elites provide the reason for the lower-class to continue to be aspired to want what they don't need. Take job security for instance, what's so secure about working long hour on a manual labor every day making money for the upper-class who is benefitting from your labor? Wouldn't it be better if we're all living in nature, catching our own fish and planting our own vegetables, so that we could spend time with the people whom we love, with the hobbies that we like, and the with the ideas that we cherish?
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: The upper-class elites have created the system that makes us feel as though we need to get a job, work hard, and pay bills so that we could forever be slaves for their service. What's certain about that kind of certainty is the chain around our hands and legs. I also think "certainty" is an enemy of growth. There're so many brilliant people (some of whom I know) who had stopped working after they're certain about their "secure job." For instance, there's a professor whom I'dearly love who has basically stopped working and doing anything academically meaningful after he had received his tenure -- or a lifetime contract to teach -- from his university. "Certainty," in this sense, leads to complacency and eventually to smugness, self-satisfaction, self-congratulation, and self-regard.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: In his new mega-hit podcast Revisionist History, Malcolm Gladwell talks about the concept of "generous orthodoxy" or the idea that'should one want to follow one own sense of morality -- what one believes to be right from within -- one needs to re-think the role, teaching, and of course, "certainty" associated with the institution that has defined your life. At the end of the day, most of the forms of certainty are associated with conventional institutions which do not change much over time (therefore "orthodox"). The definition of a moral life is the life in which one is determined to be "generous" about being on the side of uncertainty when facing backwardness defined by this conventionalism. I highly recommend that you listen to an entire podcast (there are 10 episodes in general) -- especially this episode! So, certainty is not always good.

text: This was the secret reason why I like Don Draper in the first three seasons of the TV series Mad Men so much -- he refused all attempts to get him to sign any permanent employment contracts because he believes that once he's settled he's done as an advertising genius. 2) Uncertainty: Ok. this is where I tend to agree with my friend Tony Robbins. In fact, I think the reason behind people's interest in traveling (which is the umbrella of today's largest global economic activities) as the German philosopher Johann Wolfgang von Goethe, once said was to find new experiences in unfamiliar places, just so that the productive uncertainty would be experienced. To me, personally, challenges and variety are one main quality that urges me to still want to get up in the morning.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
Although I hate surprises in general (like, when my brother sent me a message that my father has just passed away) it would be boring if everything is so certain. I like the fact that there'll be new things for me to look for in my email inbox every day. There'll be challenging problems for me to solve on a daily basis. And that there'll be things that my ability to help to contribute, either to help alleviate or improve, would be appreciated. Because I'do not care about getting recognized for any of the achievements but will take full responsibilities for the failure should it help to teach the public a lesson, I feel that my love for uncertainty is intrinsic and is not determined by the outcome.

In other words, uncertainty is what I want my life to be challenged with because I believe that which is helpful to my ability to live like a human being who is principled yet flexible, and idealist yet practical. Generous orthodoxy that led to uncertainty? [/caption] 3) Significance and recognition: I personally think that this is the quality that'shall be suppressed. As I have written about, half of the problems we have in the world have to do with miscommunication.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: And guess what? Those miscommunications are often, if not always, results of one party wanting to be recognized rather than communicating the message. I have encountered many instances that the communication broke down because one party did not want to accept any messages that we'ren't implying that which party was "doing the right thing," or "operate with a good intention," or "should be rewarded," and, the very worst, "so right that this conversation should not even take place at all." I think Robbins is absolute right that many people want to be recognized and that we should understand this fact in order to make the world a better place through giving these people what they want, namely recognition, but I also I think that we should also try to push it one step further: To teach the world that the need for recognition is a root cause of many problems. Recognition is purely symbolic, and many philosophers including the ancient Taoist master Laozi had reminded us more than two millenniums ago (he's a contemporary of Socrates) that the more we try to be symbolically recognized something, the more we will lose the grip on what we think it is. This is simply because symbolic objects, thoughts, or qualities cannot be held constant.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
They change all the time -- I'mean, literally all the time (and this idea is vivid also in the writing of David Hume about the self). An attempt to recognizing something symbolic is the same as an attempt to recognize the quality of the water in the creek. The moment you want to recognize how clean and clear the water is, that water who'se quality you want t recognize is already running down the steam and the water in front of you is no longer the same water who'se quality you want to recognize. You can only compare the general idea of it -- the general cleanness and clearness of the water in the creek -- which cannot be held constant. As in a person, the moment you want to recognize the quality of yourself, you are no longer the same person who'se quality you want to recognize.

In my earlier post " 16 things I never have," I wrote about how I have never won any competitions nor received any awards in my life. One of the reasons for my seemingly unrewarding life is the fact that I rarely apply, self-nominate, or get myself involved in anything that would lead to an official recognition. I just want to add my personal story here. Our brain consists of about 100 billion (100,000,000,000) neurons, which are specialized cells transmitting nerve impulses (also known as a nerve cell). Hey, those are about ten times more than the population of the world today.

text:
What if my life is just one of those 10 billion neurons in someone's brain? And that person is just a person in a world of billions of people. In other words, what all of this -- what we know as life -- is just a joke? This feeling scares me because it would mean that there is no meaning in any of this thing we call life what'soever.

In order to deal with this feeling, we have to think, do, and act on things as if everything is an end in it'self. 4) Love and connection: This is something that is very important to me. Although I think that loneliness is a powerful force in life, I think that a meaningful connection with someone outside of yourself is a true need. Loneliness is a productive space that'sometimes plays a significan't role in helping you to grow since coming to terms with yourself -- the person with whom you would be spending most of your time -- is the same as coming to terms with your own reality. text: I'might not completely define love and connection as "needs," as I also believe in a stoic life in which one has to always prepare to be alone and in the state of bare life -- which is the state of life from which we come and at which we will eventually arrive. In other words, loneliness helps us to come to terms with death so that we won't get so nervous when it's arriving but caring and connection gives us the meaning so that we would want to prolong the state of living as much as we are healthy enough to reciprocate their love and care. 5) Growth: I think it's true that in business, one has to "grow or die." text:

(Getty I'mages)[/caption] I, too, wholeheartedly disdain gossip. It all began when I was young. My father was a kind man; so it's no surprise that I barely remember a situation in which I was being yelled at by him. Yet, I remember the one and probably the only instance very well. It was because I told him what I thought I knew about my classmate -- gossip about his cheating at an exam.

My father immediately asked me, " How did you know for sure that he cheated? Did you see it yourself? Did you'report it?" My response was clumsy: I told my father that I "didn't actually know." And, in fact, I heard about this cheating from another classmate who was probably told about this cheating by another classmate. That was it for my kindest father: He didn't snap (I would have if I we're him, knowing what kind of man he always was) but he said to me: "There's no room for gossip in this house." He went on to talk to me about the destructive power of gossip. In fact, I remember well how he said: It's your responsibility to report this cheating if you have the evidence that it happens.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
We do not accuse people without any evidence. On the other hand, if we have the evidence of a crime and don't act, we are no different from being a criminal ourselves. That's why I asked you How did y ou know for sure that he cheated? Did you see it yourself? Did you'report it?

So, like my father, I believe that gossip is a sign of cowardice, immaturity, insecurity, weakness and lack of moral capacity to act on one's own. It's also a sign of envy and unconstructive self-doubt. I'll write more about these unwanted qualities in a person in the later part of this post. If I we're to continue with the pontiff's metaphor of terrorism, gossiping is nothing less than throwing a bomb at someone at than walk away with no responsibility (the point which I will get back to later). [caption id="attachment_4567" align="alignnone" width="2048"] My kindest father and me.

text: When two parties we're in conflict with one another, despite the power dynamics, things often get solved more effectively when those two parties engage in a direct and transparen't and form of nonviolent communication (which I occasionally had the pleasure of moderating). title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: Just because gossip tells anthropologists about the tension between members of a particular community -- and therefore makes "juicy ethnographic data" -- it doesn't mean that these anthropologists have the solid moral ground on which they could build their case in pursuit of knowledge to encourage their informants to engage in the act of terrorism against one another, especially just to "get more juicy ethnographic data." An anthropology colleague summarizes it vividly: Gossip often harbors malicious intent. Frustration at others can often boil over into anger and malice leading to gossip. Anthropologist should understand frustration, anger and malice, but not encourage it. That is, it would be like getting someone to throw a bomb at each other so that you can, in the name of the benign anatomical science, study how their bodies disintegrate. There're many other ways that anthropologists can learn about a certain society.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: In my own anthropological research, I have never sat through a session of gossip. Well, there might be a few instances or two that my language skill wasn't good enough for me to know whether I was about to be listening to gossip. But when I am fully conscious and in control of the command of the language used in the conversation, I always make sure to my informants that I am only interested in hearing from them their perceived experiences, and not their personal attacks on other people. Sometimes I let my informants share with me their feeling of discontent (that's why friends are for, isn't it), but it's known among my circle of informants that I would interject with a question "How do you know that?" should the sharing begin to incorporate any forms of gossip. This strategy occasionally urges my informants to re-evaluate their assumption and prejudice.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
It's also good for my research to make sure that my informants know that I am not the kind who is fond of gossip -- as those who is fond of gossip tend to judge, take side, and engage in malicious action of coward betrayal. What would your position be in a community that you study if all of your informants think of you as a gossip's collector? In other words, in the language of nonviolent communication, I am only interested in "evaluation-free" prose. Many might argue that this is not the right way to do ethnography, but I somehow insist on it. I sincerely do not want my view on anyone in the community where I study to be clouded by the personal evaluation of the informants who might like or hate one another.

Of course, once the close friendliness with the informants is established, my informants and I are prone to share with each other something deeper information-wise. They would ask me about my personal life, e.g., my love life and whether or not I am married. My answer would also be evaluation-free, such as, "No, I'don't have a girlfriend." And if they insist on knowing whether there's anyone, I would tell them right away. There's nothing to hide among friends, I believe. That said, I always tell my informants that it's more important for me to learn about what actually has happened or is happening, rather than what they think who did what.

In my research, I have other methodological means, such as participant observation, to understand those without having to resort to gossip. So, it may be true that my ethnographic data is not as "juicy" as it could be. I can live with it. By writing this blog post, I hope to convince you, with all my knowledge, why gossip should have no place in the society in which we are living in today, and why all of our effort would help to make that happen. I will prove to you that gossip are like bullets from an automatic gun; once you let it be heard by your ears, you will be wounded the same way you let a bullet hit your body.

Your judgement will be clouded with biases, which will lead to many decis and my dearest mother for inspiring this blog post. How did it all begin? Humans don't always gossip -- what we always do is telling stories. In a griping prose, the writer Jonathan Gottschall tells us in his bestseller The Storytelling Animal: How Stories Make Us Human about how we have always been prone to create, make up, and piece together stories. "We are wired for stories," said he.

text: Archeological evidence of ancient paintings and scriptures on the wall are evidence of that. Gossips, in many ways, are unproven speculations put together based on some grains of possibility observable in real life. That might explain why we love to justify hearing them.

text: Anthropologists and historians argued that'some sort of structure of power emerged and the sense of repression rose at some point in the short history of our existence (compared to that of the universe). Long story short: Those who'saw themselves as being in the weaker position began to communicate, among themselves, their anxiety, fear, and discontent toward those whom they saw as being in a superior position. In his best work Weapons of the Weak Everyday Forms of Peasant Resistance, the political scientists James C. Scott coins the term "weapons of the weak" to describe gossip as one of many weapons used in the manner of "hidden transcripts" by the weaker population against the superior power to whom the the weaker population have no "official" source of gauging and voicing their discontent with each other. Scott argues that "gossip as hidden transcripts" are important because otherwise the individual who'sees himself/herself as weak would no clue whether he/she is the only one feeling that way.

text: So, by gossiping, these individuals get to gauge the landscape of discontent. The argument that Herzfeld's predecessor the anthropologist Max Gluckman has made in his monumental paper "Gossip and Scandal" published in 1963 is similar to mine: Because unlike an open discussion, the closed-circuit nature of gossip create the kind of "exclusiveness" that plays a role both in indoctrinating a new member to the social group, unite the existing social group, as well as preventing any strangers from the group. In this sense, it's the weak who needs the function of gossip to stabilize the existence of social group to give them some sense of security. Gossip, on the other hand, also have the function of maintaining the morals, unity, and values of social groups for which those who consider themselves to be more powerful also have an important role to play.

text: The argument here, however, lies in the idea that those in the powerful position know that the weaker population (which can be those with the younger, new, or those with pre-designated lower tier membership) are aware of their inferior status and is actually more susceptible to "informal activities" that define the unity and membership of the group. These information activities are obviously not written in their job descriptions but are essentially to their not being a victim of vicious gossip. text: Because they know that even though their bosses cannot use their absence from these social activities to fire them, they can easily be prejudiced against because of their lack of participation in informal activities that is understood by the norm as uniting and defining the membership of the group. text:
Why are gossip so bad? Gossip create an image of a thing, person, and event before you yourself experience it. This image is what'social psychologists believe to lead to a kind of systematic prejudice commonly known as "confirmation bias," which is a kind of mental shortcut that we use to make most of the decisions in our life. Say, you have heard a gossip about your newest office's colleague "being lazy." And say you don't have any other information about this person. On Monday's morning, you're then asked to mentor this new colleague.

It's very likely that you would take the gossip that you have heard about his laziness as a heuristics of "mental shortcut" in dealing with him during his first assignment with you. You will be likely to watch him carefully and try to find any sign that confirms your bias about his laziness, and it's very likely that you will find that'sign -- not just one and many. Why? text: When communicating you thoughts: Only speak of your observation -- and refrain from your evaluation. text:
So, let me tell you a bit about the background of this design thinking series. For most of 2016 and 2017, I had many opportunities to conduct workshops on this very topic, which I found to be speaking directly to the question that I had been having for many years: How could I use years of theoretical knowledge that I had learned from school in real life? Design thinking came to me first as a surprise. I, too, taught that it's one of those terms that people liked to name-drop because "it'sounded cool," without knowing it's true meaning. Only after a few months of a truly deep dive, I realized that this was a gold mine.

In fact, I personally had long been doubting my own ability to be an individual in the world. In many posts prior to this, I talked about how I felt that the educational and socials system in which I was born we're too rigid for m e, to the point that I, at several points in my life, felt as though I'did not have a lot to contribute to my community. It's until I had recognized some of the key principles of design thinking (about which I talked in my Day 76 post ) that I began to see myself, retroactively, somewhat as a natural design thinker. Hold on right there. I am not saying that which was a good thing.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
To be a design thinker, as I wrote in my last post, one has to constantly challenge himself/herself with the idea that there's always a possibility for things to be better than it is. In other words, one has to see things the way they actually are, and not through the lens of acceptance by the majority. [caption id="attachment_4583" align="alignnone" width="3081" height="2054" alt="_MG_9595"] Photo courtesy of TCDC Chiang Mai.[/caption] For instance, I saw I was a horrible good student throughout my formal education simply because I'didn't always good grades (actually, almost never) and I had never won any awards at all. The society thought me as an ordinary majority, who would probably go on to become a much-less-than-extraordinary employee in a mediocre company. That's the way things we're presented to me through the lens of acceptance by the majority -- so, what psychologists would call -- "social proof." Then, I asked myself to take a look at the situation differently.

The question I had for myself was, What was the reason for me to feel no inspiration at all for what I was studying? Hence, the bad grades and hence the award-less student life. That's exactly the time when I realized that I was starting to see things the way they we're. If anyone we're to study what he/she was passionate in, there's very likely he or she would do much better than. Wouldn't you agree?

So, instead of hitting the internet and search as many available and existing syllabi for courses with a similar name -- and perhaps learn from those in designing my own course -- I started with finding a nice spot in my apartment's compound where I could see the trees, smell the glass, and listen to the singing of the birds. These we're the elements that made my apartment in Poland sort of a heaven on earth even though no other amenities we're there. Then, I visualized how should creativity be taught. By doing so, I wrote this following paragraph that became the basis of this course: This is an experimental class. There are three reasons behind it's experimental nature.

First, the idea of studying creativity seems like an oxymoron. A fixed set of conventions may provide guidelines for what might be useful to learn but it'should not overshadow the creative process that learners should design for themselves. Second, as design is not about thinking about what to design, but one step beyond in not only conceiving but also producing it to test out in reality, the best methods to deliver design education should also be similar. This aspect of learning, therefore, requires a high level of flexibility from both the instructor and learner. Third, design require a focused chaos ; hence, we will rely on our intuitions to deliver the best possible learning outcomes based on that.

text: This class meets once a week for a 1.5-hour session that will consist of an interactive lecture, learner-oriented seminar, spontaneous workshop depending on the week, and extensive design homework. After the first session during which the instructor will give a general overview of the particular session and some thinking points, most sessions will be devoted to discussions and hands-on experience of creative process. The close reading and critique of required texts are essential to the preparation for each session, so students are required to have read all the required readings before each session. The experimental nature of this course would require students to use design thinking and design research methods to critique and analyze special topics and assignments given to the students for the workshop session each week. Finally, don't always think that design thinking process has to involve high-tech and advanced stuff.

text: For me, this was the key. I used to think that I would need to do all sorts of things to make a course with the name like "design thinking" sound and appear super high-tech. It's not until my mother told me that "if you want your carry-on luggage to be distinguishable, why don't you just put a colored-ribbon around the handle?" Day 79: On Design Thinking Part 3: The Black Swan

September 11, 2025
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: First, this is not about the film Black Swan starring Natalie Portman, but the book The Black Swan: The I'mpact of the Highly I'mprobable by Nassim Nicholas Taleb. But what does Taleb means by the black swan? [caption id="attachment_4606" align="alignnone" width="1356"] The Black Swans in Australia. Photo: The Conversation.[/caption] It means, as the subtitle of the aforementioned book suggest, something that "seems improbable, but possible. He derives this term from a story of how we humankind believed "all swans we're white" until 1697 when some explorers, for the first time, saw black swans in Australia.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: "I'mpossible," was the feeling that these explorers had, but since they saw those black swans with their own eyes they had to believe it. [caption id="attachment_4604" align="aligncenter" width="665"] A paperback version of The Black Swan. Photo: atradernotes.com [/caption] So, in the design thinking course that I had the pleasure of teaching last semester, I'did not introduce this concept until the last 4 weeks of the semester. I was not sure that the book and the concept of black swans (which is arguably one of the most important concepts in risk management today) would be useful to design. Thanks to the lively discussion with my students, I was confirmed energetically by the interaction throughout the 1.5-hour seminar on this very concept that it's crucial for design thinkers to understand the impact of the highly improbable.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
One of the design concepts that I have yet to allude to is "resilience," or the idea that'something that is well designed should be able to recover quickly from the difficulty or robust in difficult situations. If the design is resilience, it will be popular. Think of something as basic as tiding the ribbon around the handle of your carry-on luggage to help you identify it when coming out of the conveyable belt with many other look-alike carry-ons. This "design thinking" is more resilience than a $500 bluetooth-operated carry-on luggage -- for which to work you would need batteries in both your smartphone (yes, you'll also need a smartphone) and the the bluetooth-operated carry-on it'self. You would also need to spend time taking the battery out of the carry-on upon your getting through the security gate.

Finally, it will only do the job that the ribbon does despite it's much higher realm of technology. You'll still lose it if someone steals it, or it'still won't be arriving with you if the airline misplaced it. What about for a company, city, or an individual to be resilient? Since we don't have the superpower to predict the future, what we need is the ability to be less vulnerable to the improbable difficulty. Kodak, as a company, invented many things.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
The reliance on the market for film blinded the company from seeing the then less-productive child --the digital camera technology. For that reason, Kodak got pushed out of the market by surprise when most people decide that they would go for the memory chips instead of films. So, as Taleb sees it, we need to be resilient in order to survive the unexpected. He also coins another term for this form of resilience -- an antidote to the black swans -- called "antifragile," which features also in his book Antifragile: Things That Gain From Disorder. Although I really like this term, that's another topic for another time.

For this post, let's stick to "resilience" rather than "antifragile" for now. [caption id="attachment_4607" align="alignnone" width="713"] A paperback version of Antifragile. Photo: The Conversation.[/caption] Let's get back to the black swans. I want to use the three most vivid examples that Taleb uses in the book to illustrate what kinds of things are considered highly probable. First, 9/11 attacks -- it goes without saying that we could have prevented it if we thought that it was highly probably.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: The problem that Taleb outlines is that the 9/11 attacks make many of us feel as though we could sort of almost predicted them, which is only because they are explained retrospectively. So, the first practical thing to learn about the notion of the black swans is: When the improbable takes place, don't look back at the past in pursuit of justifying why it happens (as it only looks justifiable after the fact); so, do quickly move on and be antifragile (dang, I just said let's stick to resilience). To show you more why 9/11 attacks we're only explainable retroactively, let's look at the second example: "the turkey:" Consider a turkey that is fed every day. Every single feeding will firm up the bird s belief that it is the general rule of life to be fed every day by friendly members of the human race looking out for it's best interests, as a politician would say. On the afternoon of the Wednesday before Thanksgiving, something unexpected will happen to the turkey.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
It will incur a revision of belief. Right? Just because it was well fed every day, the turkey was under the impression that everything was under it's control -- it might even thought of it'self as important whereas the bigger power over it operates at a much higher and completely different ontological plane. How many times have you been in this kind of "turkey situation?" For me? Plenty, plenty, plenty (I thought I was fed to strive, but at the end I found out that I was fed to be consumed -- metaphorically).

One more example. Third, you: I'magine a speck of dust next to a planet a billion times the size of the earth. The speck of dust represents the odds in favor of your being born; the huge planet would be the odds against it. So stop sweating the small stuff. Don t be like the ingrate who got a castle as a present and worried about the mildew in the bathroom.

Stop looking the gift horse in the mouth remember that you are a Black Swan. So, we are all black swans. Definitionally, we do not have any clue about our birth. Even if we could trace one step back to the meeting of our biological paren'ts: What brought them together and made them decide to have a family? In other words, you are thrown into the world as a black swan.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
Now we know what the black swans are. How do we deal with them? Taleb argues, and I hereby add practical advise to his argument in red, three main things about the black swans: Black swans are improbable, and often almost impossible, until they happen (just like the "discovery" of the black swans in Western Australia 1697). Don't beat yourself up too much if you happen to suffer a lot from it because you we'ren't prepare for it. You are not alone.

Think of the way to be resilience, think of stoicism, think of antifragile and quickly and robustly learn from it in order to rise up as fast as you can again to tackle the existence and aftermaths of the black swans. In design, we cannot always come up with something exclusively "for the black swans" since we don't know when they are coming (and more importantly what they are); yet, this doesn't mean that you cannot be somewhat prepared. The same reason we will never know for sure what would be the best antidote to which of the black swans in your life, we also do not know which one of our ideas previously thought as useless might become useful. So, never throw away any notes, drawings, post-it notes, sketches, and prototypes of ideas that you have. Systematically organize them in a shelve, or scan'them and organize them on your computer.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
They might not seem to be useful now, but you may need some of them in dealing with black swans; Black swans cannot be explained by the past -- think of the turkey story. So, don't try to make sense of it. What you could do is to make yourself as resilience as possible. This might mean: Don't get over-consumed by data, take small risks to test the water, do not take any big risk (putting all the eggs in one basket), and try your best to keep your option open and welcome. In design, too, this can be practiced by thinking about the variety of ideas that you are investing your time and resource in.

For instance, a lot of people who get laid off from the company for whom they have been working for years often do not know what else to do or how to make a living afterwards. The closing of the company or the lay-off can be the black swans but those who'stay afloat after the fact are resilience. A practical advise: Always have a small pet project on the side -- something that you are interested in and feeling good about when you engage in. Did you know that WeChat (China's largest social media + mobile payment network) was sort of a pet project of the mother company? There're many of these examples.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
Work on your pet project and enjoy it, as it might become handy to you. There are always unexpected factors involved in just about anything. For example, there's one time that I was so focusing on an exam, which I hoped to ace. I have read all the books, set an alarm on my phone, and I even let many of my friends know that they must call me should I not be present at the exam room at least half an hour before the exam. That morning, well, truth be told, there's a problem with the main electrical circuit board of my building, so my phone was not charged and therefore was out of battery, which meant that no one on whom I relied could call me.

A practical advise? Do not set your alarm clock on your phone; get a real alarm clock from IKEA. Sorry, that was a joke. I would never do that to you. The problem here is that there're always more factors that we thought we have already covered.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: One way of looking at any problems is to expect that'something might not go as planned (like a stoic) which may include visualizing some worst-case scenarios beforehand and walk through in your imagination, with positive thoughts, about how you might go about and fix it. Moreover, I think those who tend to be at the worst receiving end of a black swan that happen in this way are those who have been criticizing others something that if they could see it through this very lens, would know that it could well be them who make the mistakes. So, a practical advise is, and I shall quite Dr Jordan Peterson, "set your house in perfect order before criticizing the world." And since the idea of anything that is "perfect" is probably just a fantasy, just don't criticize and focus on your very own pursuit and care with great care and carefulness. In my life, there're many times that I suffered greatly from black swans. I can'tell you the most impactful one: The death of my father.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: He was 62, and just retired from work for about one and a half year. My father, at the time, lived in Bangkok with my mother and brother. He was no Hercules but he seemed to not be in any stages of seriously bad health, which I found out later that he was not well at all (and he just didn't want me to know). As for me, I was only a few hours away from my very first class of my doctorate. I had a wonderful girlfriend and was happy to be in the US where I called my intellectual home.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
My father was planning to come visit me in the States with my mother and we had a series of grand plans on what we wanted to do. Everything was pictured-perfect (at least to me, the turkey), until I receive an nerve-wrackingly short text message from my brother: "Dad passed away." [caption id="attachment_4567" align="alignnone" width="2048"] The photos, again, of me and my father. Circa 1983.[/caption] My father, in this story, was the farmer, and I was the turkey. He had an operational assumption that it would be unlikely for him to live as long as to visit me in the US, and he had subtlety made this gesture to some of his close friends, such as excusing himself from a wedding of one of his former co-workers, not attending an important gatherings and meetings, and procrastinating on packing up for a health check-up at a hospital. He knew that he was about to leave us but he didn't want me to be distracted.

So, even since he passed away, I always do three things -- in the spirit of design thinking. Go back home to visit my friends and family whenever I can. I'make it a routine. During my time at home, I would not let anything else distracting me from my precious time with my family, especially my mother; Now or never when it comes to meeting someone I'd like to see. We tend to postpone meetings that we do not see immediate needs.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
But how do we know that which are going to be a great meeting that changes your view (i.e., a positive black swan) or the last meeting between you and that person since we sincerely do not know when are we going to leave this world? Call, text, and keep in touch with those whom I care but do not have the time and resource to see them. Sometimes my friends ask me why I send them random posts or photos, I answer "to keep in touch," and that which is precisely the reason. Thinking of what they would be interested in seeing and reading is a practical act of compassion that reminds you of your value and friendship. Like good gifts, according to Robert B.

Cialdini, this very way of keeping in touch, though seen as weird by some friends (to which they eventually got used), are meaningful, unexpected and customized. Sometimes I get to strike a conversation with someone I haven't seen for decades -- and see them in person (thanks to the above now or never rule) for a coffee right after I share with them those random posts.

Day 80: On Design Thinking Part 4: Design, Fast and Slow

September 12, 2025
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: As the title suggests, I am going to talk about design in terms of "heuristics" and "general probability." Wait, wait, wait, don't close this browser yet. Those two terms may seem like jargon but basically what they mean is rule of thumb (for heuristics) and statistics (for general probability). We owe this very important domain of knowledge to two psychologists: the late Amos Tversky and the Nobel Laureate and Princeton Emeritus Professor Daniel Kahneman. Yes, it is the same famous genius Amos as recounted by the New York Time best-selling author Malcolm Gladwell in D avid and Goliath: Underdogs, Misfit's, and the Art of Battling Giants -- Tversky Intelligence Test was: "The faster you'realized that Tversky was smarter than you, the smarter you we're." In addition to being a genius (in the sense that Amos was almost always able to see things the way they we're) Amos was a hardcore optimist. In fact, I quote him most of the time whenever I need to see things clearly: When you're a pessimist and the bad things happen, you live it twice." In fact, there is a sort of psychological theory that'shows that you are actually likely to make that mistake that you think you would if you are a pessimist -- it's called a "negative self-fulfilling prophecy." I recall how I thought I would fail an exam because I was so bad at this very subject.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: I was anxious during the exam because of that thought, and lo and behold I failed and had to re-sit that exam. Looking back, that exam wasn't even that hard. My anxiety gave me the sense of uncertainties, which later on became the root of my emotionally immobility and therefore inability to focus to the exam task at hand. In other words, if you are a pessimist you would worry once about it and the second time when it happens. Sadly, because Amos prematurely passed away in 1996 (at the age of 59) before another genius Daniel received the Nobel Prize in Economic Science based on the work that the two had done together over the three decades (I know, I know, it's actually not one of the original Nobel Prize's categories but it's a good substitute).
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: Otherwise, it'd be no question what'soever that both of them would be the joint-winners of the Prize together (a wonderful biography, and also one of my favorite books of all time, of their collaboration is Daniel Lewis' Undoing Project: A Friendship That Changed Our Minds.) [caption id="attachment_4615" align="alignnone" width="988"] Amos Tversky and Danny Kahneman at Stanford in the 1970s. Photo: Medium.com.[/caption] In probably one of the most important books in the field of psychology (and probably the most readable one) Thinking, Fast and Slow, Kahneman takes us through a tour-de-force of, what has been known for half a century as, "cognitive psychology." He shows us the anatomy of consciousness, telling us where we are actually not as smart as we thought we are -- but that's also not all that bad given that'sometimes evolution doesn't always pick the smartest ones. Kahneman's key idea is simple yet complex. Let me give you two tests to prepare you for that. The first one is a classic and it comes from Rolf Dobelli's The Art of Thinking Clearly (also another wonderful book that I highly recommend).
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
Grab a pen and quickly jot down your answers to the following three questions. These are problems that are not designed for a calculator or a deep calculation, but your commonsense: In a department store, a Ping-Pong paddle and a plastic ball cost $1.10. If the paddle costs $1 more, how much is the ball? In a textile factory, five machines take exactly five minutes to make five shirts. How many minutes will it take one hundred machines to produce one hundred shirts?

A pond has water lilies growing in it. The flowers multiply quickly, each day doubling the area they take up. If it takes forty-eight days for the pond to be completely covered with water lilies, how many days will it take for it to be half covered? Don t read on until you have written down the answers. Don't spend too much time thinking about it.

If you're like most people, the quick intuitive answers that come to mind are: 10 cents, 100 minutes and 24 days. And these answers are all wrong. You could go back to those questions again, and this time you could use the pen and paper to do some calculation if you'd like. The correct answers are 5 cents, 5 minutes, an 47 days. I'll show you just the first question.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
The ball costs 5 cents, and therefore the Ping-Pong paddle, which is $1 costlier, would cost $1.05. The bill for two items would be $0.05 + $1.05 = $1.10. Actually, we don't even need to use those pen and paper at all. Why did most people think that the answer was 10 cents. Then?

Here are another two questions that do not require any calculation and come from my own experience of teaching a design thinking workshop. [caption id="attachment_4616" align="alignnone" width="800"] QR code wedding gift - Opinion. Photo: China Daily.[/caption] When was the QR code invented? (you know, one of those codes that you can use your phone to scan and can do almost everything with it these days?) Who invented it? Well, if you're among the majority of people, it wouldn't take you more than a 2-3 seconds to answer the first question.

What's your answer? 5, 8, 10 years? And for the second question, if you are like most people, you would have pretended to think as if you actually knew (because somehow you have heard it from somewhere?). At the end, if I insist that you give me the answer, you probably would not mind using your phone to look it up on Wikipedia. Did you get the answers right for both of them?
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
If you are among the majority of the people, you would not have thought that the QR code was invented in 1994 -- yes, 28 years ago. Second, you would not have any idea who invented it, let alone for which reasons (I'll leave that to you to find out on Wikipedia). Here, why most of us think we know the answer to the first question without having to insist on looking it up on the internet? In Thinking, Fast and Slow, Kahneman argues that there're two fictional systems that we use when we think. We tend to use our "hot" automatic system to make a decision, which for most of the time would work.

This hot system is precisely the one that helps you quickly recall when was the first time you saw anyone's using QR code and calculate how many years that was. This hot system also has many academic names, such as emotion (for David Hume), nature (for Jean-Jacques Rousseau) and id (for Sigmund Freud). The cold system, on the other hand, ponders over the question. When you have to think about something that you do not have the answer off hand, you would resort to this cold system to help you evaluate, argue, and therefore make an optimal decision. The cold system (Kahneman calls it System Two, as opposed to the hot System One ) often only works when it's obvious that System One won't do the job -- as the question of who invented it.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: Neurons in your brain will work very very hard to find out whether you have the answer, and if we don't the System Two won't pretend that we do and will tell us to find out on your laptop or your phone. Kahneman argues that we humans are prone and tend to make mistakes when we use the hot system when we actually should be using the "cold" system, which is almost always better than the hot "automatic" system, to make a decision. This is especially the case when we are to make a decision under some pressure and uncertainties, during which it's very likely that we would let the worst system of the two to do the job (when that's the last thing you would want). The only problem with the cold System Two is that it is, unfortunately, kinda lazy and we usually just resort to whatever comes quicker to mind -- like that answer of when QR codes came about. In fact, we have many casual terms for this hot system, such as "gut feeling," "rule of thumb," and "hunch." Where and why are we so confident about this hot system?
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: As the writer Malcolm Gladwell eloquently points out in Blink: The Power of Thinking Without Thinking, it is because this system is based on our empirical experience and it can be pretty awesome especially if it comes from an accumulated knowledge over time. Truth be told, it's this system that keeps humans alive all these years. When most of us see a snake, for example, we should thank our hot System One that tells us to "run" from it instead of activating the cold System Two to want to spend time pondering whether the snake is poisonous. There's also other uses of this hot system. In the first chapter of Blink, Gladwell discusses how the experts' cold system fails to detect a counterfeit feature of a statue.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: Only when those experts rely less on their scientific findings from the statue but on their "gut feeling," they would be able to tell that "something doesn't feel right." [caption id="attachment_4624" align="alignnone" width="723"] Kouros, an ancient Greek statue of a youth that was about to be purchased by the Getty Museum. The statue that looks and feels "too fresh" in Gladwell's book. Photo: ValueWalk.[/caption] What does this thinking, fast and slow, has to do with design? Because people are prone to use their hot System One to make a split'second judgement of what they like and do not like, design has to somewhat appeal to this very system. When asked, the Nobel Laureate Richard Thaler and the father of what is known today as behavioral economics argues that "People are not stupid; it's just the world is hard." So, according to a survey by Columbia University decision researcher Sheena Iyengar, we make more than 70 decisions everyday.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: Some of which have the potential to lead to a life-changing outcome. Wait a minute, 70 decisions??? That's a lot of decisions, according to Iyengar these decisions range from whether to take the usual route to work despite the traffic, or take a new route that you would risk getting lost and be late at work; to whether or not to ask your colleague next door out for lunch because it'd be a polite thing to do so even though you would rather eat alone because that's your habit and you'd enjoy your meal better that way. " The world is hard," as Thaler argues; it would have been much better if there's one way to get to work that is always free of traffic and that you and your colleague next door can speak your mind freely about what you would like your lunch to be like. Decisions, by definition, are paradoxical in nature, otherwise you would not have to make them.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: You have to choose one and every time you choose, there's a path that is not taken that could lead to a totally different outcome. And sometimes that/those path(s) that are not taken would give you the sense of regret. The term "paradox of choice" coined by Barry Schwartz comes handy into the circle of popular lexicon in business, management and philosophy. Schwartz shows that the more choices we have to choose from the more likely we will regret those that we end up not choosing. In fact, our obsession with choice thanks the doctrine of liberal economy (i.e., the more choices they are the better it is for the consumers to choose liberally and at will) encourages us to, unfortunately, seek that which makes us feel worse off after that choosing.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: Cognitive psychologists have shown us how limited our cognitive capacity is; hence, we'resort to the quick intuitive system based on your gut feeling (sometimes quickly rationalized as the cold System Two ) to make those decisions. Well, I have been thinking that perhaps this very concept can be applied to design too. Many of us tend to think that we know and have solutions to things that we don't because of our hot, automatic, emotion-based, and "blinkish" System One. We tend to think that you can or can't design based on the split'second decision that'should not be used frequently to come up with a meditated solution. Before Kahneman, we might or might not already know that gut feeling is not always our best bet.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: But it's Kahneman who first scientifically shows us that our judgments tend to improve if we'remove our gut feelings. This is a truly meaningful knowledge that I always recall whenever I encounter any difficult situations -- for whatever I was about to do to mitigate the situation, have I looked at the question and the situation enough with the cold System Two? Because the gut feeling relies on your emotion before you have thought clearly about the problem, Dr Marshall Rosenberg, the father of the theory of non-violent communication, would also argue that the use of the hot System One to respond to a difficult question is a recipe for a violent communication. So, most designers will lose our jobs if humans are always rational in the economic sense. We, in the words of Kanehman an Tversky, "are NOT intuitive statisticians" so we cannot always see through the big picture of things (i.e., the law of large number), and are always well prepared for the unexpected, unfamiliar, and unacquainted.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: As we know, that's not the case. We tend to prefer the expected, the familiar, and the acquainted; which explain somewhat the difficulty of any new products, services, and experiences into the new market. There're so many instances that a great product failed to stay in the market because it did not appeal -- customers couldn't connect to it. So, the designers' task here is how do you design something that appeals to the intuitive system of the users so that the users would like to give it a try, and hopefully, to like more of it. Here are the three main findings after having used Thinking, Fast and Slow in my design thinking class last term: First, Simplify, simplify and simplify -- The media and digital designer theorist and guru John Maeda writes in The Laws of Simplicity "The simplest way to achieve simplicity is through thoughtful reduction." Simple design tends to work better with human's limited cognitive capacity.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: Things that "look" and "feel" overly complicated tend to tricker the hot System One to refuse it off hand. Look at these remote controls below, which one would you want to hold in your hand and use? [gallery ids="4617,4618" type="rectangular"] Second, disentangle "truth" from "attitude" -- when we use the hot System One to make a decision for us, we are prone to fail to distinguish between what is the truth and what is just an attitude. Almost always, when you use the cold System Two to carefully evaluate a statement such as "That's impossible," you'll find out that that which is actually possible, but perhaps because it has never been done the idea of which automatically goes to the defensive mode of being called "impossible." There's a time, not so long ago, when it's understood as the "truth" that the Mainland Chinese was and would always be the cash-based society. Credit card companies found it hard even to sell the idea of credit cards to their prospective customers.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: It's believed as the truth that Chinese people only trusted the materialistic aspect of cash -- if it's not red and in your hand then it's not money. Today, China is the fastest growing cashless society. One can walk out of the house for months without having a single Chinese Yuan and live a good life -- they can even buy a car or a house using their smartphone! This was mainly because the pioneer of cashless payment in China understood that the idea that "Mainland Chinese was and would always be the cash-based society" was "just an attitude." Chinese did not trust the idea of credit cards, let alone third-party companies to whom the banks could resort in building cashless ecosystem, so they did not want to risk losing their money into the cyber clouds. So, the first thing that this company did was to strengthen the financial security between the banks, rigorously test out the system in various condition, and eventually show it to the prospective customers how it work in reality -- using the "first rule of simplicity" as a guide.

text:
When my father passed away in 2010 -- the black swan event of my life -- I was left with nothing much for which to live. I was not just devastated but also purposeless. All I felt was deep depression and that perhaps I could try to end all pain, the same way those who couldn't handle it anymore usually did. I often couldn't sleep, and when I could I had a nightmare full of terrifying thoughts, from which I would get up only a few minutes afterwards with a sweaty palms, forehead and back. In fact, I found the writing about one particular moment in the life of Eckhart Tolle resonates directly with how I felt back then: One night not long after my twenty-ninth birthday, I woke up in the early hours with a feeling of absolute dread.

I had woken up with such a feeling many times before, but this time it was more intense than it had ever been. The silence of the night, the vague outlines of the furniture in the dark room, the distant noise of a passing train everything felt so alien, so hostile, and so utterly meaningless that it created in me a deep loathing of the world. The most loathsome thing of all, however, was my own existence. What was the point in continuing to live with this burden of misery? Why carry on with this continuous struggle?

text: I could feel that a deep longing for annihilation, for nonexistence, was now becoming much stronger than the instinctive desire to continue to live. text: Recently, I have been writing a series of posts on design thinking. In the ideal world, we would all preferable prefer System Two, but the only problem is that System Two, as the psychologist Daniel Kahneman writes in Thinking, Fast and Slow, "is lazy and the mental effort is aversive." Only after a few months after my father's death that I had begun to see things differently. First, my mother was worried because I looked suicidal. text: I'llustration: Surgeon.lk[/caption] The process, needless to say, was a not a painless process (and since I have never had one myself I could not say much about my "experience" or the "memory" of it).
