Both psychologists we're able to show that the group of patients who had gone through a longer operation, which presumably meant that they had to endure more pain, could walk out of the examination room feeling "less painful" about the operation than those who went through a much quicker process. How could that be? Anchoring the "memorizing self" was what they call the " peak end rule," a mental shortcut in which we judge any particular experience based on how it was felt at it's peak.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
So, since the most painful part of the colonoscopy was the end of the process when the instrument was moving. Leaving the instrument in the bodies of the patients without moving for three extra minutes though yielded an uncomfortable sensation actually did not involve pain; therefore creating a "memory bias" that the overall process was not as painful. To those from who'se bodies the instrument was taken out right away after the operation, they'reported the process as "painful." The patients who'se operation time was three minutes longer, therefore, tended to remember the process as "not as bad as they though," compared to those who'se "memorizing self" remembered the peak of the pain as the peak end of the process. So, in this sense, if I try to use this theory to understand my pain toward the departure of my father. What was the peak-end of my father's death?

As I wrote earlier, my father passed away unexpectedly in Thailand, while I was away in the States. I'did not have a chance to say good bye. I'did not know much about his sickness. In other words, there's no closure. Would I feel less pain if I had mentally prepared myself for his death?

There's a "black swan" kind of incidence during which Ilan could have been killed but he survived the war. But a year and a half later, in March 1975, five days before he was to be released from service, he was killed in a plane crash.

Lewis writes in The Undoing Project: A Friendship That Changed Our Minds about Kahneman's state of mind: After Ilan s death, Danny couldn't help but notice the urge in those who loved him to mentally undo his plane crash. Many of the sentences that came from their lips might just as well have started with the words if only. If only Ilan had been released from the Air Force a week earlier. If only he d taken charge after his pilot was blinded by that flare. People s minds coped with loss by drifting onto fantasy paths, where loss never occurred.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
But this drifting, Danny noticed, wasn't random. There appeared to be constraints on the mind when it created alternatives to reality. If Ilan had still had a year of service remaining when his plane crashed, no one would have said, If only he had been released a year ago. No one said, If only the pilot had the flu that day If only Ilan s plane had been grounded for mechanical problems. For that matter, no one said, If only Israel had not had an Air Force.

Any of those counterfactuals would have saved his life -- but none of them came to the minds of those who loved him. Again, was this the operation of System One or System Two? Day 82: How not to "Choke"

title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: I have received a lot of wonderful comments from my last post: The Power of Now. In this post, I want to combine the 12 rules for life and the power of now, in order to lay out clearly why and how the life we are living, no matter how difficult it'seems, is not all that bad. In a few words, the real enemy here is what the psychologist Daniel Kahneman (and by now you should know how much I admire him) calls "focusing illusion" an important concept in cognitive psychology to which I have previously alluded. We tend to "think too much" about something that is usually not as important as it -- but only seems important because you happen to be "thinking" about it. In fact, many people "choke" (i.e., a nickname for the act of failing something miserably when you almost win ) when they're focusing too much about winning -- when they should be focusing on the very drive, chip and putt that they're about to perform.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: [caption id="attachment_4661" align="alignnone" width="792"] Jean van de Velde in 1999 British Open during which he "choked" so badly after almost winning the trophy with 3 strokes ahead. Headlines all over the golf world is "The Collapse." Many analysts argue that he "choked" because he wasn't able to focus in the "now" but on the British Open trophy on which his name had already been pre-engraved. Photo: doglegnews.com[/caption] Confucius then uses the example of an archery contest to illustrate the harmful effect of consciously focusing on extraneous concerns: If you're betting for broken bit's of potsherd you can shoot with perfect skill there is nothing at stake. If you start betting for belt buckles, you become worried about your aim. By the time you start betting for gold, you're completely petrified.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: Your actual skill is the same in all three cases, but because of the relative value you place on these objects, you end up paying more attention to extraneous things. It is always the case that those who focus on the outside become clumsy on the inside. We couldn't ask for a better summary of the problem facing the athletes and performers. Such people have fallen out of their usual, they have become alienated from the goals, the values, and the "flow" of play. It is not that their actual physical skill has changed, it's that they ve allowed concern with externalities to make them clumsy on the inside.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
The above passage comes from a gem of a book Trying Not to Try by Professor Edward Slingerland, using an ancient Chinese way of looking at our ability to focus. When you do not pay your complete attention to the "now," and focusing on the idea of winning, that idea of winning becomes the focusing illusion that makes you think that which is so important -- more important than the action you're about to engage in. When that happens, you happens to "choke." the renowned researcher on this topic (and also the current President of Barnard College) Sian L. Beilock argues: And the idea is that if you normally devote lots of cognitive resources to what you're doing and being in a pressure-filled situation robs you of those resources, you can't perform as well. The writer Mark Mason writes vividly in his The Subtle Art of Not Giving a F*ck: A Counterintuitive Approach to Living a Good Life: Chances are you know somebody in your life who, at one time or another, did not give a fuck and then went on to accomplish amazing feats.

Perhaps there was a time in your own life when you simply did not give a fuck and excelled to some extraordinary height.

Rhode Island School of Design (RISD) President John Maeda and the author of The Laws of Simplicity. So, how do we bring all of this together. Based on my years of working with creative minds, the solution is simple: Diversify. That is, the more you can get people who have other ways of seeing involved, the more you can come up with better strategies to enhance your competitive advantage. So, here're the practical solutions.

First, when starting a project, form a group that consist of people from, at least, three different disciplines. Of course, for example, to design and build a house you'd need an architect, a civil engineer, and electrical engineer and so on. But these disciplines are not considered as diverse -- as they're all "technical." They're the defenders of practicality. Now, imagine if you could get an artist who could conceptual the mass, volume, dynamics, and quality of the interior space. I'magine if you could get a writer who understands where best to put a desk.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
I'magine if you could get a chef who would know very well the essential circulation of a kitchen. These are "experts" in their own light who can help you to form a different way of thinking about a house. Now, if you imagine something less concrete, say you would like to re-write a policy, regulation, rule, or something. "If you only have a hammer as a tool, you'll see everything as a nail," the old saying goes. Your policy, rule, and regulation will likely to be one-sided if you think alone.

One time behavioral psychologists only think of behavioral change in terms of punishment and reward: give people what they want when they do the right thing and punish them when they don't. Maybe you'll come up with this similar "punishment-and-reward" scheme to get people to do things that you deem appropriate, but what if "nudging" -- or motivating the right behavior by ways of cognitive psychology -- is another option that is simply more effective both in terms of behavioral change and cost? Second, show your project idea to a child. This might sound absurd but believe me you'll learn something rather intriguing. text: If I we're to write my own list of " what I should have known before turning 20," the top of the list would have been Stoicism. In fact, it might have been the only thing on the list -- as the philosophers Zeno, Musonius Rufus, Seneca, Epictetus, and Marcus Aurelius have summarized it all for us on the very topic of "how to live a good life." Wait, wait, wait, I am not selling you stoicism as a philosophy of life here in this post (although I am actually very tempted to). My goal in this post, as the title suggests, is to show you how and why this 2,000+ years old way of thinking could enhance our way of designing in the 21st century. Let's start with why I think I would have lived a much better life past my 20 had I known about Stoicism back then. I'must have alluded to my love for Stoicism somewhere in the past 80+ posts, and for sure in an interview (in Chinese) that I gave to my former student, I'did talk about how Marcus Aurelius' personal note to himself complied as Meditations has completely changed my way of seeing the world.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
Over the past few months, I have been re-reading Stoicism from the perspectives of Zeno, Musonius Rufus, Seneca, and Epictetus, through which I have learned so much about what I'missed reading only Marcus Aurelius' Meditations. [caption id="attachment_4679" align="alignnone" width="776"] My very first exposure to Stoicism. The work of one of Roman's greatest emperor Marcus Aurelius' Meditations. [/caption] When dealing with the death of my father, for instance, I perhaps would have been much more calm and accepting had I used, a psychological trick that the Stoics calls, "negative visualization" to pre-live the world without him in it before he passed. I would have been much more prepared, and therefore less shock when it happened.

I would also have cherished every moment with him more; hence, wouldn't feel so much of a sense of regret. I would have also thanked him for the 62 years my father's contribution to the world (and 29 years to my life) rather than his absence. And the list goes on. In other words, had I been a Stoic, I would have dealt with that black-swan-kind-of-blow much more calmly. First, a disclaimer I am not philosophy major student and my reading of classical Greek and Roman is non-existence.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
So, my knowledge of Stoicism here is second-handed, based on what I have read from the four books below. [gallery ids="4674,4675,4676,4677" type="rectangular"] I'm going to get right into it. These are the 4 things I learned from Stoicism that I wish I had learned at least 16 years earlier. The single most important idea of Stoicism is "know what's in your control." Rule#1: There're only two things in life you can control: Your opinion and your value. Marcus Aurelius once said: You only have power over your mind, not outside events.

Realize this, and you will find strength. This rule, I believe, is the foundation of everything that has to do with Stoicism.Everything else is not in your your total control. So, knowing this very important fact, we should live a worry-free life about the tennis game we are going play, the result of the job application we have just submitted, the opinion of other people on you, etc. Those are things that you do not have the total control; hence, they're not worthwhile your time; Rule#2: Opinions of others should have no effect on you. Insults, gossip, rumor and hateful words can be sources of misery, sadness, grudge.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
We all sort of unrealistically want the world to like us -- however absurd that'sounds. I have seen so many people who cannot get over the fact that'someone just criticized them -- someone whom they don't even like! When a Stoic is asked how he feels about an unreasonably hateful opinions from his oppositions, his respond is simply (as paraphrased by the philosopher William Irvine) If he [my opposition] disapproves of what I am doing, then what I am doing is doubtless the right thing to do. Again, other people's opinions are not one of the two things that you can control. Rule#3: Seek joy from what you already have, rather than from what you don't.

Many people misunderstand Stoicism. See, even in a respectable dictionary, the definition of being stoic/stoical is "Enduring pain and hardship without showing one's feelings or complaining: he taught a stoical acceptance of suffering." This dictionary and many people think that Stoics, therefore, must be sad, emotionless, and perhaps also quite joyless because they always have to endure pain and hardship. On top of that, Stoics do not seek or expect pleasure, fame and fortune to come their way. Wait, no pleasure, no fame, no fortune ---- what a tribe? In fact, the Stoics already know that most of the time the reason why we are not happy is precisely because we constantly seek and expect pleasure, fame and fortune.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: When we get what we want, we are happy -- but only for a short time as we need to find more things to fulfill our continuing desire. And as these thing are sought after not just by us but by many others (hence limited in number), their availability is never entirely in our control (back to Rule #1); hence, they're likely -- very likely -- to be the source of unhappiness, joylessness, and discontents. So, instead of wanting what you don't already have, and using your physical energy and willpower on things you cannot control, "look inwardly" at things that you can control (Rule#1: your value and your opinion) and use them to your own advantage.You can simply change your value and opinion to like what you already have. Isn't that much easier? So, Stoics are not emotionless or sad; quite the opposite, they are always joyful because they are happy with what they already have.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
Rule#4: Focus on the means, and not the end. Wait, so does that mean that Stoics are usually poor and powerless? Since they don't seek and expect pleasure, fortune and fame? Well, no. In fact, quite the opposite: Marcus Aurelius was one of the greatest Emperor of Rome (and therefore the most powerful person in the western world); Seneca was a man of letters (in addition to being a statesman, a playwriter, a tutor and a private advisor to Emperor Nero) who was, among other things, also the richest man in the Empire; Musonius Rufus and Epictetus ran their own top educational institutions (so they would be like President of Harvard and Princeton today).

These we're all, in the strictest sense of the term, quite ambitious people, if not down right overachieving. The list of Stoics who are also successful is actually very long! How did they do it? Well, these guys we're successful precisely because they we're not doing things purely to seek pleasure, fame and fortune. They're doing their jobs because they "enjoyed" doing their jobs -- either because those jobs we're their duty to the society, or they simply "loved" their jobs.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: Again, since outcomes of anything are never in your control (Rule#1 again), focus your mind on the task that you enjoy is the best path way to success. When competing (be it tennis, exam, or whatever) you can control your mind to focus on "doing your best" -- whether or not you'll qualified for the Wimbledon is no longer in your control. Even if the success doesn't come, you're still not sad about it because you're neither doing it for the success, nor you're having any expectation about it (rule#3). [caption id="attachment_4678" align="alignnone" width="739"] One of my favorite Stoic comic of all time. Artist: RGJ (as in, Richard Jolley).[/caption] Now, these rules, if you could apply them well in your life, should already make you an employee/student/colleague of the month.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
These are qualities that everyone expects from you as a person in the society. You'll be the kind of employee/student/colleague who is not only happy with the job assigned to you, seek joy from what you already have, never complain about opinions of others, and, most importantly, hold dearest your own value of how you would like to be in the world. In a way, if you can'try to apply these rules i your life you are using design thinking to "re-design" your existence. Knowing what you can and can't control, you will be able to face with anything, including those moments when you are looking down at a frightening bottomless hole into which you are going to jump. In other words, Stoicism is an antidote to the black swan.

If we're to push Stoic one step further, what about design thinking? Turns out I am not the only one writing about this. There're quite a few great advise. Let's get right into it -- three more rules; stoicism for design thinkers! Rule#5: Try to live like a not-have, every once in a while.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
Yes, that's right. Stoics are known for their "negative visualization" -- imagining what's the worst thing that can happen. Well, the answer to that is obviously "death," but that wouldn't do you any good since you cannot simulate your own death. How, how about "poverty?" Even Seneca, the richest man on earth during his prime, would allow himself to live like a beggar every once in a while. His reasons?

To know the condition of the poor for whom he hope to serve, and to simulate the condition of hardship that he himself, despite being an upperclass man, might also have to face if things didn't go as planned. For us? There's a saying that "innovation grows out of necessity." When you're dealing with less, you automatically relocated your cognitive capacity to things that matter (remember how Steve Jobs wore the same clothes everyday?). Plus, you'll get to experience the kind of world that you otherwise (without this volunteering to live with less) would not have been able to experience and empathize. The author Nassim Nicholas Taleb would call this "being anti-fragile." In the sense, "being anti-fragile" is to be opposite of being fragile.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: Who wants to be fragile? If you don't want your entire life and soul to be rocked by a black swan event, you'd need to get ready for it; in this case, it may take some simulation of a black swan event to make you the opposite of fragile. Try this yourself, try walk out of the house without your smartphone for a week; an you'll find out by yourself what aspects of that'smartphone that you miss the most (as for me, I thought it was the camera, which as it turned out, I'didn't miss one bit; so I was very very wrong to use the quality of the camera as the single most important factor for my latest purchase of a smartphone...). The music channel MTV did precisely this with their famous "Deprivation Study" that resulted in something quite spectacularly awesome. Rule#6: Don't just talk about it, do it.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
In design practice, we have magic word that we like to say when we're getting stuck. "Show me." Instead of debating with one another or in the team how possible/impossible your idea is, we'd just pack up and go the workshop (wood shop, metal shop, etc) and just prototype our idea. That's the "show me," instead of "talk about it" principle; and usually it works like magic. Stoicism is not a theoretical philosophy. It doesn't ask you to analyze everything epistemologically, linguistically and ontologically before you can go ahead and make use of it's wisdom -- it presents you with the most reasonable, ecological and resilient solution to whatever problems you are facing -- sometimes even before you're facing them.

So, take it to heart that you don't have to "think" to live a good philosophical life. Epictetus once said: t explain your philosophy. Embody it. You, also, can just do it. Rule#7: Fail fast, and get up quickly.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
This rule is particularly important to design thinking. We want our first prototype to "suck" so that we know what works and what isn't. It's unfortunate that we're living in the era whereby people think that the more time you spend "failing," the less likely you'd make it to the top. Look at all the awards to be given specific to the "young talents" or those "under 40s" -- what's the obsession with young age? Stoics do not have any time to care about these nonsense.

They understand, again, what we can and can't control, and see through it how everyone has our own pace in learning, and finally, achieving whatever we want in this short lifespan on this earth. Having a goal is in our control -- as a goal is an opinion -- so the Stoics are fine with that, but at the end of the day, whether or not one could arrive at that goal is no longer something that is in your control, so let it go. The best way of achieving your goal is to fail fast, so that you can identify what doesn't work, fix them, and try it again. In other words, in the words of Marcus Aurelius, "What doesn't kill you makes you stronger." Alright, so I hope I have convinced you the beauty of Stoicism and how we can apply them in design thinking. One more thing.

text:
A bonus rule: You we'ren't meant to procrastinate. Stoics don't believe that we should waste our time on anything. They believe that we all have our duty to fulfill. Even if you don't have a job, you have the duty as a human being to be productive, if not to other people than to yourself. So, a practical advise?

Never allow yourself to roll around in your bed for more than one minute. After that, get up and make your bed (research shows that the very sense of completion of making your bed gives you a jump start of the day). Do everything you need to do to get yourself ready to work -- even if you don't have a job. Brush your teeth, have a cold shower (to get your blood flowing), and wear something nice (even if you are working from home, never wear a pajama and be at your desk!). Get to work!

[caption id="attachment_4688" align="alignnone" width="744"] A typical Thai noodle stall. Photo: Thai Blogs [/caption] So, let's see if we can use the simplicity of Four Noble Truth to a tackle a design problem. There is dukkha -- a chronic unsatisfactoriness of unenlightened existence. Let's say that in design, dukka is our dissatisfaction with whatever "conditional problems" that we think we can use design to solve. In this case, "conditional problems" have to do with the conditions of a particular situation.

Something that works in one place/context/operation might not work in others because of such different conditions. So, keep that in mind. We might call this process an "exploration." There is a cause of dukkha. It is dependent upon certain causes and conditions. Now that we know that there is a design dissatisfaction, the next thing that we must do is to find out what is the real causes and sources of that condition.

text:
This is where you'd need more than yourself to help you think. As I wrote in my previous post, you need more diverse eyes to help see and understand what might lie at your blind spot. In design splint, we conducted ethnographic research, brainwrite, and brainstorm to find the true causes and sources of the problem along with thinking about practical solutions. Let's call this process "define and ideation."; There is a cessation of dukkha. Yes, bad designs should not even be called designs and should come to end.

It's we who will put an end to it. But how? In the previous post, I'discuss the Stoic's use of the "show me" approach to design. So, instead of endlessly discussing how we are going to put an end to a bad design, we might as well "just do it." This is when we use rapid prototype to test out the ideas. title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
Let's call this process "prototyping (a series of problem-solving potentials." There is a path leading to the ending of dukka. In Buddhism, this is when the "Eightfold Path" comes in. To have all the "right" things in place (i.e., action, livelihood, effort, mindfulness, concentration), dukka cannot find it form in your consciousness. But how do we know that what we do is "right?" Exactly. The point here is to constantly and mindfully observe your action, livelihood, effort, mindfulness, concentration; so that they will constantly and consistently be right.

Enough or Buddhism, what about for design thinking? We, too, need to constantly and mindfully observe our own deign process. accepting the possibility of having to return to repeat the previous steps should they been done too abruptly or too mindlessly. We need to accept the chaotic nature of a design process and accept that the "right" design process is that one that involves complexity and not a straight line of progress. Whatever you thought you have done right from the first step could be wrong or misleading; so, instead of fixating on your "perfect" prototypes, you must observe the process and be able to make the decision constantly return to the precious process to enhance your design in order to arrive at an implementable product, service, or experience.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
Let's call this process "design implementation." [caption id="attachment_4687" align="alignnone" width="790"] A depiction of the design thinking process. Please notice it's' chaotic, convergent, and iterative nature. Photo: COMPOSITE is curated by Nathan Nalevanko. [/caption] Now, finally, I have to admit that I have been using this Four-Noble-Trust-disguised-as-design-thinking-process to deal with many problems in life, together with Stoicism. The most important point here is to never let your fierce emotion or the automatically hot System One take over at any point in the process.

Your fierce emotion and System One are likely to push you away from the design path that you have designed for yourself. For instance, I realized about two years ago that the source of my chronic pessimism about the world -- my dukka -- was not that the world was going to hell but actually the news about the inhumanity in the world that journalists liked to report. I'didn't know what to do. I felt as though I had lost all the hope in the world; and only wanted to move to a distant place so that I would not have to deal with the society again. I also became distrustful of many people around me.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
I became paranoid of those who wanted to come close me. I closed myself in and only spoke in a small number of people. Can you believe that which was actually what I thought of as a sound solution? Thanks to Steven Pinker's book The Better Angels of Our Nature: Why Violence Has Declined, I learned that we are living in the most peaceful time in the history of humankind. Pinker emphasizes that it's the journalists that give us the "availability bias" by bombarding us with news about the horror of humanity that, sadly, sell.

So, my solution? Since the source of my dukka is not the world -- but the news -- I simply stopped watching news, closing all my social media platforms, and engage with people in the real world without any availability biases. Since then I have become a much more optimistic and happy person.

Day 86: On Design Thinking Part 8: Dealing with Criticisms

September 18, 2025

I was about to go to bed when the idea of this blog post came to mind! Actually, someone once told me about what we can physically do to arrive at a creative idea: Drink (and smell) coffee, take a cold shower, exercise, and play some intense role-playing games. I'didn't do any of this to arrive at an idea for this very post. In fact, I was just relaxing at my balcony looking over a great city of light. O.k.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
I need to get to bed so let's get on to it. First of all, nobody criticisms even though they are important. In How to Win Friends and Influence People, the management guru Dale Carnegie writes about why and how we should avoid criticizing others: Criticism is futile because it puts a person on the defensive and usually makes him strive to justify himself. Criticism is dangerous, because it wounds a person s precious pride, hurts his sense of importance, and arouses resentment. B.

F. Skinner, the world-famous psychologist, proved through his experiments that an animal rewarded for good behavior will learn much more rapidly and retain what it learns far more effectively than an animal punished for bad behavior. Later studies have shown that the same applies to humans. By criticizing, we do not make lasting changes and often incur resentment. Carnegie goes so far as to tell us to "never criticize" -- in fact, he writes, instead of condemning people, let s try to understand them.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
Let s try to figure out why they do what they do. That s a lot more profitable and intriguing than criticism; and it breeds sympathy, tolerance and kindness. [caption id="" align="alignnone" width="800"] The author Dale Carnegie and two of his most important books (which I recommend both of them to you and to everyone). Photo: E4Thai[/caption] All make senses in the context of how to win friends and influence people, but what if you don't want to win friends and influence people? What if you want to do something with your business, life, and design strategies?

The only way can ever improve is to know where you could improve. That's when critics step n. In my life, it's been my paren'ts (especially my mother) who has always been the harshest critic, to whom I always listen precisely because I'do not have any reasons to doubt her caring, honesty and love for me. My colleagues too (especially to colleagues-in-arms) are my toughest critics, and it's precisely this very reason that we're not just colleagues but also friends. These critics are great, especially before the real battle, precisely because they are honest about what I can do to cover all my weak points.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
In design too, we want tough and honest critics. The design thinking guru Tom Kelly has, in fact, written a book about how to be "good critics" -- in which he argues against the role-playing of a devil's advocate who can only point out to the worst thing about your idea. In fact, Kelly argues for these critics playing a role of, at least, one of these "learning personas": anthropologist, experimenter, pollinator, hurdler, collaborator, director, experience architect, set designer, storyteller, and caregiver. That's another post for another time, but Kelly's point is clear: We need to learn how to take/untake criticisms. But as the management guru Dale Carnegie famously said, it's not that easy for people to take criticisms.

Nobody likes to hear bad things about them. So, in this post, I want to discuss levels of criticisms and how to deal with them -- like a pro. There're two steps to take criticisms like a design thinker. The source of the criticism. Good people (people whom you trust): There is no other way around.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: You'd have to take them like free consultations. Actually, a consultant is someone hired to give you honest advise -- and criticisms on what you can do better. This could even be your teacher. It would be foolish, for instance, to hire a tennis coach and would not allow him/her to criticize your posture when hitting the ball or serving. You have to think about them as if they're advice that normally you would have to pay hundreds of thousands of dollars to get.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: So, for these kinds of criticisms, you would want to crave them and take them very seriously. People whom you are not sure whether they are that good (but they look like they are good people): These kinds of criticisms can often come from your colleagues, managers. For these kinds of criticisms, would want to take them with a grain of salt, but you would want to do it transparen'tly. By doing so, not only you are showing respect to the critic (which would be useful in a long run), but you are also leaving ample room for the possibility for that person might be "on your side." The entrepreneur par excellence and founder of Bridgewater Associates Ray Dalio has developed a deep culture of "criticism appreciation," whereby everyone in the company is craving to hear more from other people about what they can do to improve. At Bridgewater Associates, employees are utterly used to open criticism sessions in which both the critics and criticized leave behind their ranks and constructively so that the critics can openly provide the criticized their honest opinions.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
Ray Dalio also writes in Principles: Never say anything about a person you wouldn't say to them directly, and don't try people without accusing them to their face. Badmouthing people behind their backs shows a serious lack of integrity and is counterproductive. It doesn t yield any beneficial change, and it'subverts both the people you are badmouthing and the environment as a whole. Next to being dishonest, it is the worst thing you can do at Bridgewater. Criticism is both welcomed and encouraged at Bridgewater, so there is no good reason to talk behind people s backs.

You need to follow this policy to an extreme degree. For example, managers should not talk about people who work for them without those people being in the room. If you talk behind people s backs at Bridgewater you are called a slimy weasel. [caption id="attachment_4700" align="alignnone" width="2880"] The investment guru Ray Dalio. In his lecture "How to build a company where the best ideas win" at TED Talk" Photo: TED.com[/caption] People who are at present at the opposite side of the ring (but have the possibility of changing their side): As the design thinker Tom Kelly writes in The Ten Faces of Innovation, people become your oppositions because they think highly of you -- they think that you are worth it (otherwise why would they waste their time opposing you).
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
These guys are tough critics that you may want try to convince them to be on your side. The benefit of doing so is immense. First, you'll get all the research that they have done about you. Second, they will provide you how to see the world in the way that you hav never seen it, which could lead to an innovation. It s a wonderful lesson in co-opting your opposition.

Instead of being offended by their arguments, why not listen and respond to their concerns? They often have valid points. The payoff can be extraordinary. There s nothing like the conviction of a convert to boost team momentum. [caption id="attachment_4701" align="alignnone" width="850"] The design thinker from IDEO Tom Kelly.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
Photo: managementdynamics.info[/caption] People whom you don't completely trust because you don't know that much about them: Despite your initial loathe, you would still want to take these criticisms with the mind of a great "fallibilist." Because there is no harm in believing or not believing it, you would want to take these criticisms into account as if it's something from one of you caring colleagues. Even though such criticisms may sound harsh, you wouldn't feel as if they are if you could "make yourself believe" they come from your caring colleague. So, all you need to do here is to use a psychological trick to make yourself believe, so that you can look at the criticisms the way they are. They might be useful. People whom you'really really really don' trust (especially those who criticize you anonymously).

You would want to take these criticisms as if they're insults in the way that a stoic would. A stoic, in fact, would be both delighted and enlightened to hear criticisms from those for whom they do not have any respect. Why? Because this disapproving from those who'se ideas you do not appreciate is an approval that your idea is on the right track. The philosopher William B.

What should worry me is if this contemptible person approved of what I am doing. If I say anything at all in response to his insults, the most appropriate comment would be, m relieved that you feel that way about me. Day 87: The Anti-Fragile Manifesto

September 19, 2025
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
Living in the world as hard and chaotic as it is today, it's not enough just to be flexible and resilience -- we need to be "anti-fragile." Borrowing this (I think, beautifully expressive) term from the thinker Nassim Nicholas Taleb, the author both The Black Swan and Antifragile: Things That Gain From Disorder who'se thoughts I often allude to in this blog, I am referring to this term in the way almost similar to Taleb's. What does anti-fragile mean? There're three kinds of thing: Fragile, robust and anti-fragile. Fragile things break under pressure. We treasure fragile things that are beautiful, such as glassware, chandeliers and ornaments.

We put them up to show off our status, taste, class; but we obviously do not want to be like them, especially in the world today where everyone is both discretely and proudly at each other's throat. In other words, fragile things are exposed to volatility. Robust things, on the other hand, resist the pressure and survive. Robust objects don't break easily, as they are designed to resist "some" pressure. Think of a rugged iPhone case, for instance.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
It's built to give the iPhone (a fragile object) some "robustness"; so that it won't break into pieces when it's dropped vertically or accidentally kicked to the side. Robust organizations, though not keen to be frequently exposed to volatility, are able to adapt to abrupt change through it's elements of resilience. The author Ryan Holiday writes in The Obstacle Is the Way: The Timeless Art of Turning Trials into Triumph: Subjected to those pressures, these individuals we're transformed. They we're transformed along the lines that Andy Grove, former CEO of Intel, outlined when he described what happens to businesses in tumultuous times: Bad companies are destroyed by crisis. Good companies survive them.

Great companies are improved by them. But unlike robust objects that resist the pressure and hardship, anti-fragile things benefit from them. Can you think of something like that? Something or somebody that benefit's from being exposed to shocks and crises? Nassim Nicholas Taleb doesn't provide a lot of convincing real world examples in his book.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: But examples are actually plenty. As for someone, it almost goes without saying that everyone who has made it to the humanity's hall of fame never has it easy. They have been through hardships, crises and a series of unfortunate events that make many of us think "we would have just taken an easy way out already." Take Deng Xiaoping and the stoic philosopher Seneca for example. Both of them we're purged twice -- and they both returned even stronger. When Seneca was banished to a remote Island of Corsica for 8 years by Emperor Claudius of Rome, he used those years to practice stoicism; as well as the arts of letters, playwright and entrepreneurial investment, which his busy public life prior to the banishment did not afford him the time to do so.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: When Seneca was eventually allowed to return to Rome eight years later, he was able to abruptly regain his reputation, finance and became, writes William B. Irvine, the most renowned citizen of his time: the greatest living writer in prose and verse, the greatest name in literature since the golden age at the beginning of the century, and the favorite of the imperious empress." [caption id="" align="alignnone" width="819"] LUCIUS ANNAEUS SENECA, also known as Seneca the Younger, was born sometime between 4 and 1 BC in Corduba, Spain. Photo: Notable Quote [/caption] Deng Xiaoping, on the other hands, got purged twice by his political enemies. Instead of giving up, he used the time away from Beijing to garner support from those who, like him, we're politically purged. He carefully observed and learned from the two purges and was not for a minute believed that his political career was over (although most, if no all, people around him thought so).
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: Deng was not just "robust" in the sense that he adapted to the banishment, but enjoyed the purge as it gave him the opportunity to join with those who believed in his way of thinking. Both Deng and Seneca we're anti-fragile. [caption id="" align="alignnone" width="794"] Deng Xiaoping (1904 -1997), the paramount leader of the People's Republic of China from 1978 until his retirement in 1989. Although he retained some political charisma and changed China in 1992 during his famous "Southern Tour." Photo: The Telegraph[/caption] What these guys have in common isn't their ability to solider on mindlessly and relentlessly. I return to what Ryan Holiday continues in The Obstacle Is the Way: The Timeless Art of Turning Trials into Triumph: Great individuals, like great companies, find a way to transform weakness into strength.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: It s a rather amazing and even touching feat. They took what'should have held them back what in fact might be holding you back right this very second and used it to move forward. In fact, it's their ability to calmly pause to assess the damage, inspect the situation only from the perspective that is useful for the damage control, and then turn the entire crisis on it's head by using what may be, at first, the negative remnants of the circumstance. That's precisely what the pioneering entrepreneur and founder of the Virgin Group Sir Richard Branson did when his proposal to take a few million dollars loan to modestly upgrade the entertainment systems on his Virgin planes was turned down by a bank. He paused, inspect and anti-fragilely turn the crisis around.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: First, Branson re-assessed the situation and realized that the problem was not the 10 million pounds he was asking, but the bank's lack of trust in Branson's business (which in the 1990s was still infancy and overshadowed by major airlines such as the British Airways). Instead of giving up, he struck a "different kind of deal" with the major US aircraft manufacturers -- to buy 10 new aircrafts from them. During the 1990s' UK recession when no one had the gut to invest, his proposal was welcomed by the stagnated manufacturers who desperately wanted to build and sell, the underperformed bank who wanted to lend, and the austerity-averse governments of both the US and the UK who wanted to see employment go up as a result of this monumental deal. He used such an opportunity to not only stay stay afloat in the business (as in, being "robust"), but create a 4-billion-pound worth of financing to expand his business that would later go head-to-head with the British Airways. Not only was he robust and resilience, but anti-fragile.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
Branson loved that crisis, which gave him both the business and the fame. What's more, aircraft manufacturers also threw in the entertainment systems he originally wanted -- for free. [caption id="" align="alignnone" width="826"] Sir Richard Branson of the Virgin Group. Photo: CEOblog[/caption] Businesses will likely to benefit most from being anti-fragile. Black swans, by definition, hit unexpectedly and the only way one can survive is to "be like Branson" -- be able to 360-degree assess the situation and act timely.

I have seen abrupt ending of so many businesses. It's not because they're not aware of the decline, and possible crisis. They we're aware of the decline, and possible crisis -- it's just that they we're not "anti-fragile enough" to benefit from a fierce competition. So, their "crisis management protocols" are too focused on triaging, and saving the largest portion of the business. But, I have used this example before, what if your most important products are films, in the era of digital cameras?
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: We, too, I believe can benefit from the notion of being anti-fragile. How many times have we faced black swans event in life that made us think "what doesn't kill us makes us think it'should have?" Plenty of times for me. We are living in the world where it's harder and harder to predict. Like the "turkey story," there is no way that a well-fed turkey would know that his only true raison d' tre was to be killed and fed to humans to celebrate Thanksgiving. If this turkey was a stoic, it would have taken the moment of truth like Seneca who, upon being given a death sentence by his own pupil Emperor Nero (for whom Seneca had faithfully served as a counselor), proclaimed that which "was the best opportunity for him to practice Stoicism." Or, this turkey could simply have found the way to be anti-fragile and run away.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
In my line of work, I found that there're three kinds of companies: S tartups, running, and crumbling. The first and the third kinds are likely to benefit most from the concept of anti-fragility. Both the newcomers and those about to go out of business will be fierce to find their competitive edge and will therefore work hard to be more than robust. The second kind won't even realize that they need to be anti-fragile because, like the turkey, everything seems okay until the day they are about to go out of business. In my own life, there're times when I was attacked, backstabbed, and tripped by malicious people.

They thought I was "fragile" and hoped that I would simply break into pieces. Some of them, while speaking to me with their sincerest look, actually hoped that I would literally disintegrate. I'met so many of this kind of people, especially during the past decade of my life. Honestly, there're times when that was almost the case -- I almost literally disintegrated. It's only then I realized two things which saved me: First, that my paren'ts did not build me to be "fragile"; and, second, that, quite counterintuitively, there're always something that is quite useful in almost any unfortunate events.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
You just have to, like Sir Richard Branson, look for it. For example, as I wrote earlier, the fact that I was the odd-one-out in college did not prevent me from wanting to be more than just robust. My college life was really tough. None of my classmates liked me. They also thought that the disliking was symmetrical, even though I'did not have anything against them.

The truth was that I'did not have a single friend during my 6 years in college because I'did not fit with the institutional culture whereby studiousness was looked down upon (as nerdy) and laziness was praised as "a way of the wise." Most of my classmates did not go to class and spent most of their time playing soccer. Of course, I could"robustly" pretend to like my friends and lean in. That way I could have a few friends and would not be so friendless. Yet, I knew that I was going to spend only 6 years there as a student, and the real life would only begun after that. That's the beginning of my 8-hour-long reading project for the next 6 years.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
I knew that had a "grand plan ahead," although at the time it'seemed far-fetched: My teachers literally laughed at me during the only one time in my undergraduate life that I told anyone that I would like to go to the US to pursue my further study in architecture. They thought it was not possible for someone of my low intelligence and "personality" (again, my friendlessness was a big thing for them). It's not surprising that nobody understood what I was doing then. My classmates, my teachers, and even some of my friends thought that I had lost it. They thought I had become depressed, sociopathic, and "anti-social" because I had no friends.

[caption id="attachment_4706" align="alignnone" width="1280"] In college. All I'did was to show up in class and then left for my beloved libraries. I had no friends at all.[/caption] Little did they know that I was in no way anti-social, I was just practicing "anti-fragility." So, let's be anti-fragile. Let's not let the unbearable lightness of words, selfishness of humans, and disturbances of which we do not have the capacity to control in this chaotic world bring us own. Keep it'simple: Here're what to do: We'll take all criticisms like they're free consultations.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
A lot of people pay tons of cash to professionals to criticize their businesses -- it's called consulting. So, now that there're people who would give us their thoughts for free, we will take them, embrace them, and long for more. Criticisms, in a way, produce a crisis and a shock. No one else likes to be criticized -- except us, because we are anti-fragile. We love to gain from crises and shocks.

Criticisms from great minds will prepare us for a more anti-fragile life, while criticisms from small-minded people will serve to confirm that what we are doing is already, and absolutely, the right thing to do; Never put all the eggs in one basket -- EVER AGAIN. The one single most alarming characteristic shared among almost everyone who'se black swan events drive them to complete destruction (e.g., suicide) is how they do not diversify their investment portfolio -- not just money, but also time, life, etc. As we know that we are not necessarily and solely responsible for unfortunate events in our life, it's unfair to think that it's possible for you to win just because you have done your best. The world isn't that'simplistic -- in fact, it never was, is, and will. If you put all your money on one bet and you lose, you still lose even if it wasn't you who caused it.

text:
If you're still a waged labour, think about how you might turn your hobby into another job. If you're solely relying on a day-to-day income, think about how your commitment to a longer-term task might give you both the pleasure of seeing things through and a more stable income, even for a short period of time. So, diversity your finance, your time, your hope, your expectation, and your ability to believe in your anti-fragility; Regain our independence. The point is, when we are interdependent on something, especially things to which we are addicted, we don't have a lot of choices and therefore prone to make mistakes and/or let someone else's mistakes affect us. Once we're independent, we can'take a much better control of our own life. We can'try out more new ideas and work more diligently on being anti-fragile. text: The moment we'realize that which is the case, we're one step closer to being anti-fragile. Be like Bill Gate of Ancient Rome Seneca who frequently "caused bad things to happen as the result of our undertaking a program of voluntary discomfort" in order to learn how to be anti-fragile. I have moved the previous Day 88 post "Only If I Can Do These 12 Things" to the main page of this blog and changed it's name to "Principles" since these are, essentially, the principles by which we should aim to live our life. Now, in this post, I want to quickly unpack them into practical guides. Here're a few practical guides according to the 12 principles: First, be in the "flow," in other words, be here now (no, not the 1997 album of one of the favorite band's Oasis). The meaning of flow is discusses in Bill Burnett's book Designing Your Life. Flow is engagement on steroids.

Flow is that'state of being in which time stands still, you're totally engaged in an activity, and the challenge of that particular activity matches up with your skill so you're neither bored because it's too easy nor anxious because it's too hard. People describe this state of engagement as euphoric, in the zone, and freakin awesome. Have you had this experience? I have had plenty of them, usually when I'do something I really enjoy, such as teaching, reading, biking, and writing a blog post (such as this one, and yes I am in the flow right now). My advice: Do take note of activities that put in the state of flow -- and find a job that let you do that day in and day out.

text:
I think Burnett has summarized it all. Once you're in the flow, your brain won't be spending it's precious energy on anything else except the very task at hand. The key is to focus on the smallest portion of the task that you are engaging (which will prevent you from "choking") and trying to get yourself into the "flow" of whatever you are doing. Second, "don't try." The two words written as the epitaph on the writer Charles Bukowski s tombstone. In this sense, don't try to be nice by taking others' problems as your own.

Fourth, be a maximalist -- on good values such as modesty, community, love, forgivingness. For all of these quality, more is, simply, more. text:
Years ago, before I closed all my social media accounts, I asked myself one question: "Why is my life so bad?" "Good" and "bad" are relative. So, for anything to be "good" or "bad," there has to be a reference point. As you can probably guess, I used photos of other people that I saw on their social media as reference points. That is, I felt that my life was bad because I constantly compared my life to the heavily-curated portrayals of others on the internet. In The Art of the Good Life, the author Rolf Dobelli argues that "Envy has a bigger impact on your life satisfaction then physical affliction or financial ruin, and the ability to manage it is fundamental to the good life.

In the similar tone to Dr. Jordan B. Peterson's fourth rule in 12 Rules for Life: An Antidote to Chaos "Compare yourself to who you we're yesterday, not to who'someone else is today, Dobelli puts in simply, Stop comparing yourself to other people and you ll enjoy an envy-free existence. Week 89: The Saddest Sonnet Have you ever felt disgusted by your own existence? I have. My weakest days we're the ten months before my obtaining a doctoral degree in 2016. No, living alone by myself per se did not make me weak. The feeling lack of support, instead, was the source of my weakness.

In the process of finishing up "my most important work," I felt deeply insecure. I wasn't afraid that my work wasn't good enough. In fact, I knew that I would graduate anyway whether or not my work was any good at all. I'mean, unless my university was sadistic, there was no reason for it to waste more resources on a student who had reached his intellectual limit for another year. Instead, I was afraid of what would come next.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
After finishing this doctorate, I would be "forced to be free." I would be let go from the protected world of students, into the unprotected world of cutthroat employment. Making me weak was precisely that feeling of wanting to finish something, while knowing that finishing it would put me in an even more vulnerable place. Again, this being "forced to be free" thing was not the kind that a high school kid thinks about when leaving their homes for college. I thought of it in a the same sense that the French enlightenment thinker Jean-Jacques Rousseau had thought of: The stripping away of a person's inalienable rights, dignity, and sense of morality as a whole. Yes, I was that panic about being forced to be free, and the fear of which forced to be someone else but myself.

How was I someone else but myself? Let's start with a near-death experience. Have you had one of those out-of-body experience? It actually happened to me, not so proudly, quite a few times. There is even a pattern.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
Most of the time when I saw myself as a third person was when I engaged in a act of ferociously scolding at other people on mistakes for which, I knew very well that, I alone was responsible. No, I wasn't floating in the air like Casper the Friendly Ghost. And no, not true, I wasn't feeling as though the law of gravity didn't apply to me. In fact, I felt heavy, especially my beating heart. My two foots we're on the same flooring tiles on which the angry man who looked like me was.

Feeling as though I was the invisible third person (yes, this time, like Casper the Friendly Ghost), I felt as though I was looking at myself. It was almost as though the better angel of my nature had escaped the body in order to see what the worse angel was trying to accomplish with it's malicious desires. Although I'did not hurt anyone, I'did not care about others as much my own self. Even though I'did not do any harm, I'did not try to help those distant friends who we're in need. Even though I'did not cause any one any trouble, I too-consistently refused to take part in activities I knew would have brought betterment to the society as a whole.

text:
I was extremely selfish: I was trying to stay afloat. Not only did I lose some good friends during these weakest days, but I was also failing even in the very basic tasks that I was assigned to do. I'll return to the part about losing friends later. It's much easier to see how I had become a train wreck in my personal life. During these twelve months, my daily life was a mess.

Sleeplessness gradually creeped in. I tried to exercise, yes; but twice as much I had been eating unhealthy food and binge-watching television, with the hope that these could stray me away from having to think about what would happen to me the day after next. Before I knew it, I would be staying out all night musing over some unpleasant feelings. I would be staying awake looking at the white ceiling of my apartment for hours before I realized that I would need to chug a can of something (mostly beer) to get my brain to stop working. Sometimes, I used old (and classic) movies to give me some meanings of life to my attempt to fall asleep.

A bonus to me: I learned how to make organic cocktails (which I'made several videos about) so that I could pass time and use it as a bonus to go to bed (although alcohol rarely worked on me as a sleeping pill). I wrecked my body that way until it could no longer take it, and hence automatically shut down, as in stopped working at an instance and left my body collapsing on a couch in front of a fuzzy television's screen. Without enough deep sleep the night before, and because I needed more sleep then, I would constantly skip breakfast when I should be getting ready for work. Because I was far from being able to meet my own expectation, I became defensive. I felt as though the reason why I wasn't able to finish things was due to some conspiracies. I felt that I was in a war zone and my enemies we're my friends and colleagues in disguised. A cognitive behavioral psychologist later told me that was what happened when one felt insecure: Everyone became your enemy because you we're not secure about your own role, position, and, emphasized by the psychologist, "your own existence in the minds of others whom you cared." At the time, I outright rejected this assumption. I'did not believe that anyone could have any influence over my own opinion about myself.

I also did not believe that I could care about someone so much that I became neurotic. I wasn't realized that I cared so much about what other people thought about me. There was a "feedback loop from hell," which went like this: I was anxious about being insecure, and therefore wanted attention; such craving for attention led me to pursuing things that I could not deliver, which, in turn, gave me even more anxiety as a result of criticisms, complaints and grudges. text: Almost always, we're already biased by our lack of something. Just to complicates things a bit: If somehow someone happens to tells you that the iPhone really isn't good, you're very very (as in 87.5%) likely to confirm your judgement that the iPhone really really isn't good -- even though you have never touched it, never seen it, and all you know is the name of the product and the fact that "two people in the world" have said it isn't good. text:
Judging others -- let alone accusing or blaming -- based on information confirming your bias is nothing less of judging the quality of the entire Indian Ocean by the small cup of water you have just scooped up from a small contaminated river two thousand miles away from that Ocean. As I have written earlier, one of my 12 rules for life is to be content with not having to have an opinion on things about which I'do not know. This goes without saying that I am a big fan of Dr Jordan B Peterson's "set your house in perfect order before criticizing the world" rule. And since a "perfect order" is rarely accomplishable, don't criticize. Live your life in the here and the now.

Back to Tolle's words "If you are very alert, very present, you can do so without ego involvement without blaming, accusing, or making the other wrong." In short, I find the act of badmouthing other people to be intellectually, spiritually and morally a big fat waste of time. One rule still applies: Don't engage, walk away.

Freedom is one of the most difficult topics. We all want freedom, but... Freedom from what? Thaler and the author and legal scholar Cass R. Sunstein' s idea that even those hardcore "free-willers" to live a better life. That which means that you have own your rule by which you live. But what if the society forces you to eat that chocolate cake? If obeying your own rule means disobeying that of the society's, then you're only free if you engage in an act of civil disobedience. Yes, not eating that chocolate cake and being put in jail is freedom, for Kant. In addition to avoiding decision fatigue, there's another true socio-psychological benefit of turning down that'slice of chocolate cake.

text: What he's wrong (or perhaps didn't address quite eloquently, to give the great philosopher the benefit of the doubt) is that this ability to think doesn't give us the pleasure that we as a species should enjoy -- the pleasure of "being in the moment." Day 94: On Making an Argument

September 26, 2025

O.k. I just couldn't sleep. I have been having an issue with sleeping lately. I perhaps should seriously take my mother's advice not to take a nap after my late-afternoon run. Now, I am wide awake.

Even after using the secret military technique who'se claim is that it could make anyone fall asleep in two minutes, I still could not fall asleep. So, while rolling on one side of the bed (as I had this hypothesis that changing from sleeping on the back to the side helps me fall asleep; spoiler alert: I was wrong), I came up with an idea for this post. So, I want to write about arguments. Specifically, I want to write about how to have the kinds of arguments that benefit you and your counterpart. First and foremost, the rule of thumb is not to argue with anyone.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
This is one of the late life and self-help guru Dale Carnegie's golden rules in How to Win Friends and Influence People, and also, well, a commonsense. Arguing for the sake of arguing is a waste of time. So, should you argue, make sure that which is a productive and friendly exchange of diverging or opposite views that has the potential to lead to intellectual benefit's of the involved parties. Countless of time in my own life and of those whom I care, I witnessed how an argument could lead, and have led, to an end of friendship, relationship, co-workership, etc. Those are the instances whereby not having an argument at all would have been better for the parties involved.

Again, an argument that leads to an end of a friendship isn't worth pursuing. But, why does it happen? So, let's get it'straight rule by rule. The most important rule: Not just an argument (but especially for an argument) but any form of communication that is likely to be effective has to be nonviolent. I am not talking just about a direct verbal abuse (which is obviously violent), but any form of ineffective communication, such as an criticism, judgment, sarcasm, and vague statement.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: It's true that most of us get emotional when we engage in an argument, and forget that it's never as important as the relationship you have with your counterpart with whom you're arguing. So, for sure, the first thing to do is to watch your own emotion and never let it override your reason. The methods of nonviolent communication will help you do just that. I wrote about this a few times in the past, but let's reemphasize it here again. [gallery ids="4825,4826,4827" type="rectangular"] Like me, the psychologist Dr Marshall B Rosenberg believes that the main reason for most conflicts we have in the world today is our inability to communicate "non-violently." When we communicate, we don't just say what we feel and what we need from those to whom we're expressing ourselves.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
We judge, compare, criticize, curse -- most of the time without any good reason. Most of the time, argues Dr Rosenberg, we use imprecise, judgmental and presumptive language to prematurely evaluate a situation. These are communication that involves the use of negative adjectives (e.g., bad, mean, evil) and unnecessary adverbs (e.g., always, never, frequently) which are neither useful to get our meaning across, nor to get the other people to empathize with you. For instance, how many times does saying "You're always late" help to encourage someone to be punctual? Here's one of the examples from Dr's Rosenberg's Nonviolent Communication: A Language of Life: Life-Changing Tools for Healthy Relationships: A couple in distress attending a workshop provides an additional illustration of how nonspecific language can hamper understanding and communication.

I want you to let me be me, the woman declared to her husband. I'do! he retorted. No, you don she insisted. Asked to express herself in positive action language, the woman replied, I want you to give me the freedom to grow and be myself.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text: Such a statement, however, is just as vague and likely to provoke a defensive response. She struggled to formulate her request clearly, and then admitted, s kind of awkward, but if I we're to be precise, I guess what I want is for you to smile and say that anything I'do is okay. Often, the use of vague and abstract language can mask oppressive interpersonal games. The expert on marriage the psychologist John Gottman at the University of Washington also adds that one secret to a long lasting marriage is precisely the use of precise, non-judgmental and non-presumptive language. For instance, if your partner forgets to take the garbage out, it's because he "might have simply forgotten to do so" and not because he was "bad," "lazy," or "taking advantage of you." Asking your partner whether he forgot to take the garbage out is likely to receive a good response than rhetorically asking whether he is doing it intentionally because he is "bad," "lazy", or "taking advantage of you." Once you get to the very bottom of what you're trying to express every time before you're doing so, it's likely that you won't communicate violently.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
Alright. So, a nonviolent communication is the undergirding structure of all good arguments. Here're the rules (I am cheating here by taking the first 4 rules from Dr Jordan B Peterson's 12 Rules for Life ): Treat yourself like someone you are responsible for helping. What Dr Peterson means here is that many times we treat other people better than we treat ourselves. For instance, I have friends who are always there for me when I need them, but whenever they need me they never call.

They don't want to bother me. I think that's unfair. Dr Peterson's example is that we tend to make sure that our pet (e.g., dog, cat) take prescribed medicines, while we tend to forget to take our own medicines. We're kinder to those about whom we care. In making an argument, you need to be as kind to yourself as your counterpart.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
You're also learning from the process and the argument should enrich, not deteriorate, your relationship with your counterpart. Don't pursue an argument that is not helping you personally; Assume that the person you are listening to might know something you don't. I'must have heard this from one of the podcasts I listened to a while ago, but there's this line that really sticks: "If you can't first argue in the position of your opposition, you have no right to argue your own." What this rule will remind you is that while making an argument, watch out of things that might allow you to excel in your understanding of the subject matter. Again, the point of the argument is not to win, but to learn. By why do we think that the purpose of an argument is to win?

In Metaphors We Live By, the linguistic psychologists George Lakoff and Mark Johnson argue that it's because of how we have been using "war metaphors," such as "Your claims are indefensible," " He attacked every weak point in my argument," "His criticisms we're right on target," to name a few (and I recommend the book; it's a classic!). You get the idea. But that's misleading. Once and for all, we should treat arguments, not as war, but as learning process. Many of us today are trying to re-write these kinds of misleading metaphors.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
Love, for instance, doesn't have to something that we would "fall into," "hurt by" or cause "heartbreak." Using these as metaphors for love really isn't helping; Tell the truth or, at least, don't lie. Yepppppp; Be precise in your speech. Yep. Again vague and imprecise speeches, according to Dr Rosenberg, are considered violent communication. When you want something to do something (e.g., take the trash out), be precise in your speech -- in this case, what'should be done (e.g, "Could you please take the trash out," not "Why is the trash still in the house").

In an argument too, be clear in what you're trying to convey; Do some research, or don't. Make no compromise here. An argument is only worth pursuing when the two opposite views are based on some research. There's one instance whereby I found myself engaging in a debate about the economy of recycling with a colleague. It took us 5 minutes to realize that none of us knew anything about that topic; hence, we just laughed at our silliness and dropped the "argument.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
You can have a casual nonviolent conversation about something to stir up some thoughts (but not gossip though) but do not take it as an argument if none of you have done any research or know anything about the subject matter. As the Stoics would say, "it's completely okay to not having an opinion on something." If the reason why you'd rather go one restaurant over the other has to do only with your gut feeling, just say so; don't try to make up other justifications for it; "When the facts change, I change my mind. What do you do, sir? This quote is often credited to the great economist John Maynard Keynes (despite the lack of any written evidence). Keynes said so with regards to his changing view regarding macroeconomics of the UK.

Often, economists stick to their guns, and their theories; hence, for someone as important as Keynes to say this, it's not to be taken lightly. He's right. We don't know everything. Do you'remember when we all used to think that'smoking was okay? The psychologist Dr Robert B Cialdini, in fact, points that "consistency" is one of the tricks that'salespeople use to sell us things that aren't that worthy.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
That's another golden rule (so many of them, lol). When engaging in an argument, the only preparation to have is to be enlightened, to learn from it, and to change your mind if you find the argument opposing you more convincing. Avoid using the word "you" and try to use the word "I." The worse kind of fallacy is the one that is directed against a person rather than the position (i.e., ad hominem, in Latin lit. "to the person"). Using "I" makes it much easier to express feelings, and much harder to attack the person.

While it's usually useful to start an argument with "I," e.g., "I believe," "I think," "I feel" -- and you must follow it up with the magic word, "because." The psychologist Ellen Langer wrote in her classic study conducted in 1978 about the magical power of the word "because." Well, it's not that magical, but let's say that it would increase substantially the logical compliance and therefore the chance of your argument's being heard and taken seriously. O.k. Day 95: In Praise of Incrementalism

September 27, 2025
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
I am just five days -- five days -- away from the golden "100 days" mark. For those who don't know why that mark is important, please return to the first page of this blog. I have been asked by a dozen of friends, "What are you going to do once you get to the hundredth day?" I actually have not thought about that. I am thinking of two options: Going back to my previous posts and revise them (as in, "re-broadcast" or "radio replay"), and keep going beyond the hundredth day. As of now, I prefer the first option a bit more because I feel the urge to go back to my previous posts to read about my childish and immature thoughts.

My hope is to use my humility to answer whether or not I have changed from those childish and immature days. So, for the next five posts, I want to write about some the "key ideas" that make me who I am today. I, however, will skip those you already know from my previous posts, such as rebelliousness, lateral thinking, and morality. Oh, and probably romance and sex as well. I will probably come back to these at some points, but for the last 5 posts of the first 100 days, I want to talk about something less subjective, and more scientifically proven to be the case for self-development.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
Let's start with the first one: I believe in consistent and gradual change, one step at a time. Some may call it "incrementalism." I hold this principle dearest because it has been the single most important principle that has so far positively changed my life. In my previous post, I wrote about those 5,000 hours over 6 years that "got" me into learning from English textbooks. Long story short, I'didn't have any friends when I was in college; so, by default, I had to find other way to spend my time when I wasn't in classes. I ended up spending hours every single day for 6 years reading books in the library -- since books could not really say whether they would like me to be their friends (I should say "a friend," rather than friends, during my first year in college because I'managed to finish only one book during my freshman year).

Yep, I'didn't become a serial reader right away (now I can't stand not reading something awesome everyday). Back then, I'didn't write a single word. It's simply because I could not even construct a simple grammatical sentence in English (e.g., "Me loving you," which sounds like what Cookie Monster would say). During the entire first year in college, therefore, I only managed to read just one hundred-page-long book. Yes, just one book for the whole year during my freshman year.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
In 1999, it took me about three days to get through a page, on average. It was that'slow. But the process was not as annoying as I thought it would be. I still remember the joy of flipping through my old paper dictionary looking for words. That's what happened when you had to look up every single word in a paper dictionary.

But bit by bit, I was able to speed up my ability to comprehend what went through my eyes. Incrementally, bit by bit, one step at a time, I became faster at reading. Six years later, in 2005, I could finish a book in a week. "Little by little" is the meaning of "incrementalism." Just for one task -- the task to learn how to read in English -- it took me six years to do it. In 2018, I can probably read a book a day.

I have come quite a long way from then -- little by little. But I am already at least some 20 years away from those days (I graduated college in 2004), so "incrementally," I could reduce my reading time from one year to one day. That's 365 times faster -- but over the course of 20 years. This year, for instance, I hope to finish about 200 books. It's still not 365 books because I have other things that I'd like to do as well.
title: Blog Posts - Clean

text:
In fact, the author Rolf Dobelli writes in The Art of the Good Life that we should be more selective in how we'read according to science. We re reading wrong. We re reading neither selectively nor thoroughly enough. We let our attention off the leash as though it we're a dog we're happy to let roam, instead of directing it toward it's splendid quarry. We fritter our most valuable resource on things that don't deserve them.

Read a book twice? Why not? In music we're used to listening to a track more than once. And if you play an instrument, you ll know you can't master a score on the first attempt. It takes several concentrated iterations before you can hurry on to the next piece.

Why shouldn't the same go for books? The effect of reading twice isn't twice the effect of reading once. It s much greater judging by my own experience, I'd put it at a factor of ten. If I retain three percent of the content after one reading, after two readings it's up to thirty percent. So don't start stamping your reading ticket until you're about thirty, but be ruthless thereafter.

Here're the other four. First, Quality (over quantity): There's no other way to put it: It's more important to me to get one thing done right, rather than get many things done half-right. Second, Process (over result): This has been the mantra with which I often shout at myself whenever I begin to expect that I should get whatever I want as a result of the work done well.

Many thinkers, especially the Stoics, have reminded us again and again that we suffer when we try to control things we do not have any control over. Of course, you may think that your work is good enough to get a good result (in fact, as the psychologists Daniel Kanehman and Amos Tversky have reminded us, we tend to overestimate our ability to plan and therefore over-expect our ability to achieve), but even if that which is the case, you must not undermine other factors that could shape the result.
