Calvin Chu 
Managing Partner, Eden Strategy Institute
How can cities recover “greener” and 
“smarter” after having been affected 
by the COVID-19?
Smart City Ranking: Why Should Every 
City Care About it?
 As the global development network of the United 
Nations, the United Nations Development Program (UNDP) 
views smart city development as a tool that encourages 
equal participation of everyone in every aspect of society. 
Through the application of “design thinking,” the cities are 
expected to grasp a better understanding of the underlying 
problems and subsequently address the real needs of the 
people. At the same time, the use of technology will help 
facilitate people’s access to the economy, health system, 
and education, which in turn, will lead to the reduction of 
social inequality and the increase in participation as the 
basis of economic growth and sustainable smart cities in 
the long run.
 As one of the partner agencies of the Digital Economy 
Promotion Agency (depa Thailand), Eden Strategy Institute 
plays an important role in conducting research and con -
sulting leading government and leading private sectors 
across the globe. The Institute is internationally known for 
its pioneering work on “Top 50 Smart City Government rank-
ings” that explicitly focuses on the most important driver of 
smart city development or governments. During Smart City 
LEARNsS, Eden Strategy Institute had shed light on “why 
do we need to prioritize smart city rankings?” as a common 
strategy for each city would hinge upon its ways of develop- 
ment. Nevertheless, the aspects discussed during this lecture 
have increased our awareness of rankings, particularly
55
title: Smart City Week 2020 Booklet

text:
Jeroen Kok
Director, Rebel Group
Shoubhik Ganguly
Principal, Rebel Group International, South Asia
Private-Public Partnerships to Enable 
Smart Cities
 Smart city investments take many forms, depending on 
the type of project. Municipalities, for example, frequently in-
vest entirely in projects that are primarily concerned with the 
public’s interests and are less concerned with profit-making. 
On the other hand, the private sector, typically represented 
by a city development company, would prioritize investing 
in profit-driven projects. Ultimately, “PPPs,” or Public-Private 
Partnerships, are the third most well-known type of invest-
ment. PPP is frequently a collaboration between the private 
and public sectors, reinforcing each sector’s comparative 
advantage. The public sector, in particular, would have greater 
clout on policy issues and legal proceedings. Despite its 
status as a central authority, the public sector frequently 
faces budget constraints, a lack of expertise in various fields, 
and a lack of incentives to develop a sustainable business. At 
the same time, despite having greater capabilities in terms 
of technology, digital literacy, innovation, and marketing, the 
private sector frequently encounters administrative bottle-
necks from the government. As a result, the PPP is a perfect 
marriage of each sector’s comparative advantage. PPP has 
been proven to deliver tremendous success around the world, 
according to two experts from the Rebel Group. For example, 
based on their experience, the design and development 
 in the standard-setting process and the understanding of 
Smart City Ranking’s mechanism. Undoubtedly, both factors 
can genuinely drive positive impacts in various dimensions 
of smart city development; The more international tourists 
we can attract, the better we can promote tourism and 
encourage confidence-building, leading to a more sustain-
able approach to developing smart cities.
56

text:
Miguel Gamiño Jr
Executive Vice President, Global Cities – Enterprise 
Partnerships, Mastercard
Making Tech Work for People Across 
the Globe
 MasterCard is widely regarded as the world’s sec -
ond-largest credit card management firm. Today, however, 
MasterCard is striving for better transformation in order to 
avoid being disrupted by new technology brought about 
by new market players. Simultaneously, the company has 
positioned itself as a global tech company that sees oppor-
tunities in the challenges that cities face, such as health 
concerns and economic meltdown, which can primarily be ad-
dressed through multi-sectoral cooperation from all sectors 
around the world. As a result, MasterCard decided to collab-
orate with various organizations to implement projects such 
as donation platforms and contactless shopping technology. 
 Based on these motives, MasterCard has initiated the 
“City Possible” program that aims to create powerful techno-
logical tools to help improve the economy and the people’s 
quality of life. In doing so, the project will assist the people 
in connecting with a global network of city leaders, indus-
tries, businesses, educational institutions, and government 
and private organizations across the globe. Furthermore, 
another project mandate is to encourage project members to 
exchange knowledge and innovation in particular seminars. 
Finally, several workshops will be held to constantly explore 
new challenges and examine the problems facing different 
cities in the hope of implementing the most sustainable 
solutions.
 Miguel Gamiño Jr., Mastercard’s Executive Vice Presi-
dent for Global Cities , has addressed issues and challenges 
global cities are experiencing. As such, it is the intention of 
the company that the City Possible project would serve as 
a third party that is highly committed to connecting global 
city members through information and resources, which, in 
58
title: Smart City Week 2020 Booklet

text:
turn, will accelerate the pace of smart city development. 
Last but not least, Mr. Gamiño Jr emphasized that creating 
a smart city is possible through three major factors: a global 
network, urban co-creation, and a scale co-created solution 
or collaboration between tech providers.

Executive Director, Center for Inclusive Growth,

Future of Work
 People are becoming increasingly concerned about 
their jobs being taken away as technology advances. In 
reality, however, humans are constantly striving to improve 
and challenge themselves. As a result, claiming that new 
technology will replace human jobs is a fallacy. Work patterns 
must evolve in tandem with technological advancements. As 
a result, what remains critical is the development of skills 
and the acquisition of new knowledge in order to align them 
with new technology in the future.

on the Cloud
 Cloud computing is yet another remarkable technology 
in city management. Because of the highly secure and 
accessible system, cloud computing allows users to choose 
whether to keep their data privately in a personal server, 
which prevents data leakage, or to open them up for public 
use, which can be accessed from anywhere. Furthermore, 
cloud computing can alter the size of data storage based on 
the nature of the data or the needs of the users. Furthermore, 
the system introduces five game-changing technologies that 
work well in the cloud and will be the future of a better world. 
These technologies are as follows:

 Office buildings in many cities usually consume more 
than 60% of energy usage. Thus, the acceleration of new 
and modern construction techniques can help reduce the 
problem of overconsumption with the support of the govern-
ment. Notably, the government policy can monitor the cost 
of technology so that ordinary people can equally access

into the atmosphere, SCG has presented a technological 
solution to air quality that is being released from air condi-

Artificial Intelligence (AI): technology that enables computer 
systems to learn by themselves from machine learning and 
deep learning;
 1) Augmented Reality (AR): technology that connects 
the actual world and the virtual world through various soft-
ware systems and other connecting devices;

devices through the Internet;
 3) Data Lake: mass storage with a better management

Peggy Liao 
Senior Sales Manager of FETC, Taiwan
Taiwan Smart City Solution Showcase
 The widely used e-Payment platform in Taiwan serves 
as a model for many cities. In particular, all types of public 
transportation in Taiwan can be paid for with a single card, 
simplifying all steps into a single streamlined system. How-
ever, implementing this type of system is difficult because 
it necessitates commitment, a desire for convenience, and 
a determination to provide equal access to customers and 
lower ticket prices. It also entails combining psychological 
and technological developments from both the private and 
public sectors in order to create this single platform.

text:
Jamie Fu
Vice President, Global Cities, Mastercard
The Role of Technology in Realizing 
the new Normal for Public Transit
 Mastercard has introduced a technology that will em-
ploy blockchain technology in the public transport system. 
When implemented, the consumers will be able to access 
product information, trace the production line, and make a 
payment with the blockchain data. Moreover, the technology 
will improve public transport by installing a contactless 
payment system or e-tickets as one of the modern measures 
to help prevent the spread of COVID-19. However, provided 
that this blockchain technology is advanced and can be 
costly, not every city can afford it owing to concerns over 
investment, break-even points, local demand, and people’s 
openness in adopting new technology.
Ashok Venkateswaran
Vice President, New Payment Flows, 
Mastercard Labs, Mastercard
How Blockchain Can Support Efficiency 
and Governance
 “Trust” is blockchain’s keyword. As the number of inter-
net transactions increases, blockchain technology adoption 
is critical for improving efficiency and building trust. Previ-
ously, we were used to an identity verification system that 
required a hard copy of an ID card; however, with blockchain, 
all personal information would be stored in a decentralized 
blockchain system, and only the owner of the data would be 
able to access and use such data. Consider how much easier 
life will be when trust is always guaranteed. The emergence 
of this technology will undoubtedly alter how we conduct 
transactions.

This session focused on exchanging views of smart 
city design for the elderly, particularly in terms of universal 
design, convenience, safety, and environmental concerns. 
Also, it encourages and supports the elderly to have good 
health and lifelong happiness. In part thanks to the advance-
ment of technology and medical innovations, people can 
live longer with an average life expectancy of 77 years in 
Thailand, thereby making it essential for the elderly to have 
their retirement life planned, especially in the New Normal 
era where digital technology is a big part of our daily life. 
As such, raising awareness of digital media and proposing 
guidance on how to become ‘smart’ people with good health, 
recreation, integrity, cognition, and peacefulness remains a 
prominent agenda. Last but not least, support from govern-
ment agencies, the private sector, and civil society is crucial 
to help the elderly adjust to an appropriate level of digital 
literacy and help cultivate post-retirement jobs, generate 
income, or facilitate access to health and safety services. 
Consequently, the elderly would be able to grow with confi-
dence and stability in this fast-changing digital era.

text:
Smart City: Smart อย่างไรให้เท่าเทียม
Manop Iam-saard 
Manager of The Social Enterprise Development Center, 
The Redemptorist Foundation for People with Disabilities 
Suchart Owatwunasakul 
Chairman of Disabilities Thailand (DTH) 
Torphong Selanon 
President of Thailand Association of the Blind (TAB)
Siriporn Praserdchat 
Logistics Officer of Asia-Pacific Development Center on Disability 
(APCD)
 Most people with disabilities have difficulty navigating 
public spaces, traveling, and communicating in their daily 
lives. As a result, when designing and building a livable 
smart city for all, smart city development should take these 
concerns into account. Four major elements must be 
reviewed and taken into account:
 1) Smart Living – a place designed for people with 
disabilities to accommodate their activities and safety;
 2) Smart Mobility - a public transportation system that 
supports people with special needs and helps them navigate 
their commute;
 3) Smart Governance – a space that allows people with 
disabilities to participate in policymaking and decision- 
making process related to urban development planning; and,
 4) Smart People – a concept that encourages the 
change in attitudes towards people with disabilities; in 
particular, it facilitates their access to technology, which in 
turn, can lead to employment opportunities in various ways.

 The activity introduces three presentations from ‘Gen-Z

title: Smart City Week 2020 Booklet

text:
Rungsun Promprasith
CEO of QueQ Thailand
Startups x Cities: Pain Points to Solutions
 The ideas of the QQ’s application manager can be 
used to build a smart city in 3 areas. The first idea is related 
to the application design or software. Initially, the QQ was 
designed to manage queues at restaurants. However, in 
light of the pandemic, the application has since shifted 
its strategy, or PIVOT, to also operate different kinds of 
appointments, such as embassy appointments, hospital 
appointments for the treatment of patients, and visitors’ 
appointments at government offices. This shift of strategy 
turned out to be highly beneficial in reducing the risks of 
contracting COVID-19. 
 The second area is related to solution finding or 
designing products and services that address pain points. 
This focuses on the “People First” principle, product testing, 
and prototype creation, which may take several stages to 
develop and become the most appropriate models. 
 The third topic is how to behave like a “pirate.” This 
refers to how a startup use technology to bypass the red 
tape-filled process by which government agencies and the 
private sector work in concert to solve city problems.
68
title: Smart City Week 2020 Booklet

text:
Chatpong Chuenrudeemol
Silpathorn Artist, Architecture of the year 2020 and Chief 
Executive Officer CHAT Architects
 Architecture is an essential element of urbanization. The 
role of architecture in urbanization cannot be overstated. Most 
people think of architecture as the creation of new structures, 
such as oddly shaped buildings or the distorted use of space. 
In reality, architecture is a mindset or inspiration influenced 
by our surroundings that can aid in the creation of designs. 
Vernacular architecture, for example, is a style of architecture 
built with local materials, local wisdom, and local people’s 
hands. It places a premium on craftsmanship and design to 
meet the needs of the residents. Furthermore, vernacular archi-
tecture fosters community participation, activity engagement, 
and human connection.
 Therefore, becoming a smart city does not imply several 
buildings; instead, it focuses on every dimension in a city. 
Architecture is about connecting the community and patterns 
of life for the people. In the eyes of Mr. Chatphong, who led 
design research called “Bangkok Bastards” or “Uncompromising 
Architecture,” he noted that the architecture of impermanent 
housing, such as those of construction workers, slum com -
munities, or illegal markets, are stemmed spontaneously from 
“city people.” Often regarded as the garbage dumps, the sad 
reality is that they do not have access to the urban economic 
cycle, and they must struggle for their survival, living on left-
over, cheap materials like scrap metal, zinc, old wood, campaign 
signs, Fanta posters, or pictures of celebrities that are cut into 
wallpapers. However, from an architectural perspective, it is 
the art of recycling that serves the most meaningful purposes. 
Mr. Chatpong emphasized that as you delve deeper into this 
type of story, you will discover pure magic in these people’s 
spatial arrangements. It is a wonderful realization that cities 
belong to everyone.
Open House: A Call for Accessible Architecture 
and Urbanism for All
69
title: Smart City Week 2020 Booklet

text:
Social Entrepreneurship: Bridging Business, Society 
and Happiness
Peangploy Jitiyatham 
Co-founder of Locall Thailand 
Winyoo Srisupachok
We are Volunteer English Bangkok
 The link between people, communities, macroeconomics, 
and national policies can help generate businesses within a 
community. “Locall” is an excellent example of a charitable 
business that provides delivery services to local food stalls. 
Despite several challenges in pricing, competitiveness, 
and quality, Locall, with the help of other agencies, has 
helped fulfill the growth of local food providers and sustain 
their businesses during the pandemic. Another example 
is “We Are Volunteer English Bangkok,” which enters the 
community and assists locals in managing and balancing 
their school, leisure, and family lives. Based on these two 
examples, it is critical for any community-based project 
to thoroughly understand the needs of the locals and be 
prepared to address the city’s pain points.

text:
Next-Gen Agro-Commerce: Taking Thai Agriculture 
to the Next Level with Digital Economy
Dr. Preesarn Rakwatin 
Vice President for Digital Agriculture Development and Pro-
motion of Digital Economy Promotion Agency 
Krittha Bhokharsathit 
President of E-Commerce Committee of MOAC 
Thanain Toomhiran 
E-commerce and Online Marketing Consultant 
 The COVID-19 global pandemic has profoundly caused 
labor shortages in the agricultural sector due to the 
repatriation of migrant workers. The farmers are forced to 
adopt the Internet of Things or IIoT technology an innova-
tion that embeds electronic circuits, software, sensors, and 
network connections in digital devices and equipment. By 
doing so, the farmers can utilize the IoT to create automatic 
watering systems, automatic power on and off systems, and 
help distribute agricultural products via an accessible and 
affordable online platform, such as Facebook Live. Digital 
Economy Promotion Agency, as a critical stakeholder in 
smart city development, has also implemented several 
supporting measures, such as the depa Mini Transformation 
Voucher, which allows farmers to purchase digital products 
or services and assist them in applying modern agricultural 
techniques to their traditional farming methods.

title: Smart City Week 2020 Booklet

text:
Tackling Crisis like a Pro: How Medical Technology 
Take Us Through and Beyond COVID-19
Beyond the Cash World: Digital Currency and the New 
Opportunity
Assoc. Prof. Dr.Cherdchai Nopmaneejumruslers 
Vice Director of Siriraj Hospital 
Dr.Chokchai Suwanakijboriharn 
Advisor of Bangkok Dusit Medical Services (BDMS)
 During COVID especially, medical technology has signifi -
cantly helped Thai people provide public health services. For 
example, Siriraj Hospital has developed a drug dispensing system 
using automated robotic technology, a drive-thru blood collection 
service, and a telemedicine service for patients with mild symp-
toms via video call. For the latter, medicines would be distributed 
to the patients after the consultation session. In addition, the 
system also helps facilitate the patients who have been treated 
or diagnosed abroad. As we progress, the future of the medical 
and public health industry will be using artificial intelligence (AI) 
technology to do a preliminary assessment of symptoms and 
diagnose disease with fewer errors and a higher level of precision.
Jirayut Srupsrisopa
Founder & Group Chief Executive Officer of Bitkub.com
 In the future, money will no longer be paper. Instead, it will 
come in digital form. People will no longer have to carry cash, and 
they will be able to complete transactions via digital systems, as 
can be seen from Bitcoin's blockchain system that has transformed 
the world's financial system. Furthermore, banks in some countries 
have already stopped offering bank account services to their 
citizens, owing to the high costs of management, building a branch, 
and installing ATMs. With the emergence of digital currency, 
transactions can now be done via mobile applications, thereby 
allowing more people to easily access loans, personal accounts, 
or complete financial transactions anywhere and anytime.

text:
Turning a Crisis into an Opportunity: 
Living in the Changing Post-Pandemic World
Dr.Udomthipok Praikaset 
Vice President of Thai e-Commerce Association

Associate Dean for Research and International Affairs, 
Thammasat Business School
 The pandemic crisis is an accelerator of the rapid decline in 
the Thai economy. In particular, the tourism industry--which is the 
most significant contribution to the economy--has been severely 
hit by COVID-19. On the one hand, retail businesses, fresh markets, 
grocery stores have been deeply affected while on the other hand, 
the e-Commerce business is growing exponentially, thanks to the 
behavioral change of the consumers and the availability of online 
platforms. At the same time, we are witnessing the growth of 
the IT business as it highly fulfills the needs of work-from-home 
lifestyles. With these examples, entrepreneurs are encouraged to 
look for more opportunities in crises, adopt new strategies, and 
take advantage of each moment not only for survival, but also for 
a more thriving future.
“A city belongs to everyone, whether 
you are a child, a working-age person, 
an elderly, or a person with disabilities. 
Therefore, everyone must build their 
smart city because central to this 
development is the fact that the city 
must address the common problems 
and meet the needs of all people living 
in the city.”

needs of everyone.

text:
Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ) 
Q: Does it only have to be a province to be developed into a smart city?
Q: Is it necessary to develop a solution that covers all seven areas 
of smart city development?
Q: What would be the benefits of becoming a smart city? 
Q: How long does a Smart City badge last? 
Q: What are the criteria for becoming a smart city? 
A: No, two types of areas can be developed into smart cities: (a) the develop-
ment area at the provincial/ district/sub-district or municipality levels and 
(b) the area developed by the private sector or other government agencies. 
A: No, it is not necessary to provide services in all seven areas, but at least 
two areas are required, i.e., Smart Environment and any other area depending 
on the context of the space.
A: The private sector that invests in the development process will receive an 
8-year tax exemption privilege (with limitations) from the BOI. Details can be 
found at
A: It is valid for two years. However, the applicants must report the progress 
every six months within two years.
A: There are five criteria: 
1. Having clear goals and precise areas of development 
2. Having guidelines for urban infrastructure development 
3. Having a secure city data collection and management system 
4. Having smart city services in two of the seven dimensions of smart city 
5. Having a sustainable management approach
85
title: Smart City Week 2020 Booklet

text:
“Smart City”
is a word that everyone might be familiar with. How -
ever, not everyone truly understands its underlying 
meanings. What is a smart city? What does a smart 
city look like? Can smart cities genuinely improve 
the quality of life for urban residents?
 Climate change, pandemics, the growth of populations, 
and mega-urban migration have accelerated the pace of 
development in many megacities. Thus, preparing and 
handling these situations have become an essential and 
urgent matter in different areas, such as infrastructure 
management, environmental and housing management, 
social services, public health services, and education. 
Given such challenges, traditional technology might not 
be adequate to carry out innovative solutions. At the same 
time, to bend with the force of urbanization, digitalization 
has become a new dimension for urban citizens. Therefore, 
smart city development is one of the resolutions that can 
help many cities cope with their concerns and serve as 
another crucial factor of smart city development.
86
title: Smart City Week 2020 Booklet

text:
Livable City
Definition of “Smart City” 
Through technology and innovations, a livable 
city refers to a city developed in a variety of 
fields to fulfill the diverse needs of the resi -
dents. For instance, basic infrastructures, pub-
lic utilities, housing, resting areas, worksites, 
commercial spaces, and the arrangement of the 
city’s space should be developed in the most 
compatible way with the city’s identity, culture, 
and traditions. Therefore, this type of smart city 
development focuses mainly on addressing 
pain points according to the city’s needs. 
 Smart City is a city that capitalizes on modern technology and digital 
innovation to enhance the efficiency of city services and management 
systems. Such technology will help reduce costs and maximize resources, 
emphasizing a friendly design and active engagement between the business 
and public sectors. All in all, the main goal of developing a smart city lies in the 
creation of a livable and modern city, which, in turn, will improve the quality 
of life of the people and generate sustainable happiness for all.
New City refers to a city that is completely 
redeveloped and transformed into a modern 
city. Similar toLike the livable city concept, 
technology and innovation remainremains at 
the core of the development, covering seven 
areas of the smart city development agenda. 
In other words, the city should be developed 
in all fields, including basic infrastructures, 
public utilities, housing, resting areas, 
worksites, commercial spaces, and the proper 
allocation of city spaces. An example of this 
type of smart city is Wang Chan Valley in 
Rayong Province, which was built from scratch 
by PTT. Specifically, the city was completely 
transformed from a no man’s land into a brand-
new capital.
New City 
87
title: Smart City Week 2020 Booklet

text:
A city that takes into consideration the 
environmental impacts and climate 
change conditions. It focuses on using 
technology to systematically support 
water management, climate, waste 
management, and disaster surveillance 
and increase people’s engagement in 
conserving natural resources.
A city that can manage energy efficiently, 
balancing between the production and 
energy usage to foster energy security and 
reduce interdependence on the power from 
the main grid.
A city that uses digital technology to create 
added value in the economy and manage 
resources efficiently. Examples include 
smart agricultural cities and smart tourism 
cities.
A city built by taking into account the 
Universal Design, thereby improving the 
people’s health, safety, and well-being.
A city that develops government services 
to better facilitate the people and 
stakeholders in accessing government 
information and services. It places a great 
emphasis on transparency, participation, 
and consistent improvement through ser-
vice innovations.
A city that focuses on developing smart 
traffic and transportation systems to drive 
the country. Moreover, it increases the 
efficiency and connectivity of various trans-
portation and traffic systems, improves the 
convenience and safety of transportation, 
and cares for environmental impacts.
A city that aims to cultivate knowledge, 
improve skills and build an environment 
conducive to lifelong learning. Moreover, 
it intends to reduce social and economic 
inequality and foster creativity, innovation, 
and participation.
7 Smarts
7 Dimension of Thailand 
Smart City
88
title: Smart City Week 2020 Booklet

text:
Special Article: 
The Making of an Innovative 
Governmental Organization 
and the Development of 
Smart Cities in Thailand
The Importance of Cities and 
Innovation Development 
 According to the United Nations, 
a “city” is recently redefined as an area 
where more than 55 percent of the world’s 
inhabitants reside. The cause of this change 
in definition is a result of the change in 
economic systems, particularly the re -
placement of traditional agriculture and 
manufacturing sectors with the services 
sector that generates higher economic 
value. Thanks to this, the economy has 
grown by leaps and bounds. Furthermore, 
coupled with technological advancements, 
machines and automation can now, in 
some part, replace the work of humans 
with higher precision and better efficiency 
than human labor in agriculture. As a result, 
the traditional manufacturing sector is 
dwindling. Both contexts are the push and 
pull factors that cause the movement of 
urban migration for severala number of 
reasons, particularly working, study -
ing, or residing. As a result, this forms 
the centralization of infrastructure, 
economic, social, and cultural invest -
ments of the urban areas—all of which 
can be denoted by the density, com -
pactness, and cultural diversity. 
 This “urbanity” is what many 
thinkers and researchers regard as 
the source of creativity, an essential 
element of innovation, particularly of 
technological change. Provided that 
a city is an area filled with expertise, 
common problems, density, and space 
constraints, ideas and interests are 
being gathered and transformed into 
solutions, which are the main driving 
force of the economy or the so-called 
innovative ecosystem. Undoubtedly, 
the continuous development of inno -
vation fulfills the needs of the people 
89
title: Smart City Week 2020 Booklet

text:
during different times, eras, and con -
texts of society. Therefore, almost 
all innovations that have influenced 
our lives from the past to the present 
stem from this “urbanity.”
 A city with a better environment 
and higher equality would positively 
influence innovation development. Pro -
vided that the number of urban inhabi -
tants in the world will rise about 68 per-
cent (more than two-thirds of the world) 
in the next three decades, “the future of 
cities, therefore, is the future of innova-
tion development.” The development of 
such innovations is seemingly the future 
of the majority of people. In Thailand, if 
we analyze the Thai economic system, 
we could witness that the importance 
of the cities has increased over time in 
almost every area, such as society, ways 
of living, economic development and 
commerce, and geopolitics of Thailand, 
to name a few. 
 Apart from being a place of in -
cubation, a city can also be a source 
of pollution and negative externalities. 
Often, the development of a city worsens 
environmental issues, mainly due to 
90
title: Smart City Week 2020 Booklet

text:
the reckless use of resources by urban 
residents and economic activities. For ex-
ample, urban buildings usually consume 
more than half of the total urban energy. 
As long as the business system is service- 
related, these buildings would consume 
a considerable amount of power for a 
long time. 
 In addition, the source of electricity 
stems from the production of unclean 
energy with poor circulation. Thus, 
this wasteful use of electricity directly 
accelerates the greenhouse effect and 
generates various pollution problems 
such as dust, air, and water pollution. 
Moreover, it also leads the urban residents 
to turn into global consumers, causing 
harmful environmental impacts that may 
take centuries to rebuild a livable com -
monplace for all. 
91
title: Smart City Week 2020 Booklet

text:
The development of city potential is both 
supporting and nurturing factors of inno-
vation incubator and ecosystem—both of 
which are important to drive innovation 
and are the main components of city 
development. Smart City is one of the 
approaches that use technology in city 
management to fulfill the needs of the 
residents. Different types of technologies 
are needed to build a Smart City, but 
most importantly, big data. Specifically, 
it can be used to analyze and translate 
raw data into a set of information that 
facilitates decision-making in the most 
precise and targeted manner. 
 One crucial point to note is that a 
smart city is not a fixed output but rather 
a process that brings out the potential of 
a city through the continuous develop -
ment process. It starts with setting up a 
framework for the development process 
to be carried out in stages later. How- 
ever, for a city to be fully developed into 
a sustainable smart city, it should consist 
of these components:
 1) The boundaries and vision of the 
city are based on the economic, social, 
environmental, cultural, and other rele -
vant factors of the area;
 2) Smart city infrastructure invest-
ment approaches cover basic digital 
infrastructure, transportation infrastruc- 
ture, energy infrastructure, public 
utilities, and any other infrastructure 
corresponding to the goals, objectives, 
forms, and types of the smart cities;
 3) Guidelines on the development 
of City Data Platform (CDP) by linking or 
using information for management. This 
also includes providing services in smart 
city areas, cybersecurity management 
guidelines related to smart city systems, 
and the safety of personal data;
 4) Details of the of smart city 
services, including activities or projects 
corresponding to the type and charac -
Innovation and Smart Cities
92
title: Smart City Week 2020 Booklet

text:
teristic of smart city development as 
requested for consideration under (1); 
and,
 5) Sustainable management guide-
lines of smart city development in the 
form of public sector only, or a hybrid 
of private and public sectors, or other 
private sectors as designated by the 
Smart City Development Steering Com -
mittee.
 Given that each city has different 
needs, readiness, and willingness to 
develop may be the first factors to con-
sider when deciding to become a smart 
city. Overall, Thailand’s Smart City policy 
is aimed at guiding Thailand toward 
becoming a developed, high-income 
country where people can enjoy a high 
quality of life with less inequality in all 
aspects. Based on such expectations, 
governments recognize the importance 
of digital technology by prioritizing 
people’s digital literacy and assisting 
them in effectively accessing digital 
technology.
 It is important to note that “Smart 
City” development is only a process, not a 
technological outcome. Instead, the most 
important technology provides measurable 
livelihood to citizens. Therefore, it is essen-
tial that a city develops its framework and 
prioritizes the needs of its citizens. Further- 
more, if a city can accommodate the 
needs of the residents, there will be less 
investment cost, thanks to the efficient 
allocation of resources; otherwise, this 
development process could be overpriced. 
Thailand can learn from both lessons to 
determine which studies would maximize 
benefits as we advance.
93
title: Smart City Week 2020 Booklet

text:
Currently, Thailand is striving for 
“Thailand 4.0 Policy,” which aims to drive 
a value-based economy rather than a 
quantifiable one. Therefore, innovations 
that can effectively fulfill economic, 
social, and environmental should follow 
a “Bottom-Up” development approach 
or a people-centric framework. With this 
model, if the cities grow, the country 
would rise accordingly. Today, the 
development of a smart city is led by the 
National Steering Committee on Smart 
City, appointed by the Prime Minister 
of Thailand and chaired by the Deputy 
Prime Minister. They have begun to 
implement the smart city development 
plan and tasked depa Thailand as the 
Committee Secretariat to set the criteria 
for the smart city development.
 Applications can be submitted to 
depa Thailand to receive special pro- 
motions and benefits offered by the 
government, such as tax benefits offered 
by the Thailand’s Board of Investment 
(BOI), Sandbox benefits (Sandbox refers 
to an experimental area that can freely 
apply technologies) provided by the 
Office of the Broadcasting Commission 
and the National Telecommunications 
Commission (NBTC). However, the word 
“city” is not always restricted to govern-
ment agencies. Any private sector that 
can offer spaces in industrial estates, 
educational institutions, or any coopera- 
tion between the private sector and 
the city can submit a proposal to depa 
Thailand to receive support for devel -
oping a smart city. Typically, the goal 
of Thailand is to reach 100 cities by 
2022--47 of which already have been 
implemented a smart city development 
plan. More information can be found at 
www.smartcitythailand.or.th
 Despite clear visions, active govern- 
ment support, and innovation develop -
ment, the development of “Smart City” 
in Thailand has opportunities and chal -
lenges. As such, the leading role of depa 
Thailand is to ensure that those challeng-
es are addressed, and opportunities are 
protected. Furthermore, provided that the 
Promotion of “Smart City” 
by the Governmental Sector
94
title: Smart City Week 2020 Booklet

text:
Dr.Supakorn Siddhichai 
Group Executive Vice President 
Digital Economy Promotion Agency 
development of smart cities is relatively 
new, depa Thailand’s primary responsi -
bility is to create shared understanding 
across all sectors, including both on 
the demand side (or the city itself), and 
on the supply side (or the producer of 
technology). Thus, such a challenge will 
allow both the supply and demand sides 
to collaborate, experiment, and test out 
the technology.
 Furthermore, Depa has designed a 
course named “Chief Smart City Officer” 
(CSCO) to build smart city developers. 
The course focuses on building prac -
tical knowledge such as key concepts, 
rationale, and digital technology in urban 
development. Moreover, it will assess 
the project’s feasibility as the develop -
ment frameworks usually involve new 
investments, such as establishing a city 
development company and a Public-Pri-
vate Partnership (PPP). For those who 
are interested, CSCO is now open for 
registration. The course would begin 
in August 2020. To inquire for further 
information, please contact depa Thai -
land’s Smart City Promotion Department 
at scp@depa.or.th.
 Finally, to raise awareness of smart, 
livable cities both in Thailand and abroad, 
depa Thailand organized “Thailand Smart 
City Week 2020, which took place during 
October 2020. This event is part of the 
strategies that promote knowledge shar-
ing, particularly among cities aiming to 
become smart cities and technological 
providers from Thailand and abroad.
 Furthermore, the event intends to 
incorporate digital innovation into smart 
city development in order to improve peo-
ple’s quality of life. Furthermore, Thailand 
Smart City Week serves as a platform for 
Thailand to become the global leader in 
driving smart cities, particularly in the 
Southeast Asian region. Last but not 
least, the event is committed to encour-
aging collaboration in the development 
of smart city projects, particularly 
through public-private partnerships, and 
to continuing to build a stable learning 
platform, such as the CSCO course in 
smart cities.
95
title: Smart City Week 2020 Booklet

In light of the assessment of the seven dimensions of smart city, 
the working group has put forward a smart city development

Phuket
Smart City
5 Pilot Smart Cities 
98
title: Smart City Week 2020 Booklet

text:
7 
Smarts
 Phuket will have smart city services and solutions in all 7 dimensions, with a total of 42 
smart city projects. Among these, flagship projects include the comprehensive integration of 
the artificial intelligence-powered closed-circuit cameras (CCTV), serving as the city’s robust 
security surveillance system. To help reduce crime and prevent accidents, both face and 
license plate recognition systems will be concurrently deployed. Promoting community-based 
tourism through the Phuket Tourism Information Management system will also enable service 
providers to boost demand and attract visitors. In addition, there are also digital projects for 
the tourism of the adjacent Andaman coast, i.e., the so-called “Andaman Tourism Digital Twin” 
project which will help to distribute growth in the sustainable tourism economy throughout 
the region. Renowned legacy projects such as the Phuket Smart Bus will continue to operate 
synergistically with novel projects like the Mobility-as-a-Service (MaaS) platforms to serve 
both the residents and visitors.

This project aims to develop 
itself to become a livable 
metropolis and an innovative 
city that connects all 
economies in the Greater 
Mekong Subregion (GMS).
Khon Kaen
Smart City

Smart City Thailand
100
title: Smart City Week 2020 Booklet

text:
7 
Smarts
 The total number of smart city services that will 
be in operation stands at 28, covering all 7 dimensions. 
Notable projects include the maximization of green 
areas by using the Internet-of-Thing (IoT) technology to 
manage ecological resources and mitigate community 
waste problems. By using IoT sensors to monitor the 
amount and condition of waste as well as provide com-
plete analytics of the collection route, the streamlining 
of this end-to-end waste management process can 
enhance the already-world-renowned urban landscape 
and improve the wellbeing of the citizens. In the domain 
of the smart economy, Khon Kaen will continue to build 
an ecosystem for entrepreneurs, especially SMEs and 
Startups in the IoT or digital industry. Medical and 
healthcare applications for comprehensive public health 
services are being designed to foster the city’s 3M value 
proposition: Mobility, Medical and MICE (Meetings, 
Incentives, Conferences and Exhibitions). The connec- 
tivity of transport networks through information techno- 
logy, such as the light rail transit, is staged to become 
an important component of the city’s growth through 
transit-oriented development (TOD).

title: Smart City Week 2020 Booklet

text:
3 
Smarts
 Mae Moh will have smart city services and 
solutions in 3 domains, with a total of 10 smart 
city projects, including those of smart environ-
ment and smart energy which are EGAT’s general 
expertise. The flagship projects of “Mae Moh 
Smart and Pleasant City” include rehabilitating 
20,000 rai (7,907 acres) of community forests, 
building 100 water management prototypes, 
and introducing robust climate sensors. State-
of-the-art Geographic Information System (GIS) 
will prompt results for effective environmental 
management. Moreover, digital payment ser -
vices will be introduced to generate income for 
the community by promoting and community 
products. As an Eco Town, it will adopt a smart 
grid technology using a smart metering system 
and installing solar power generation on the roof 
to reduce the use of fossil-based electricity and 
therefore increase the use of renewable energy.

 There are going to be 42 smart city projects 
across all 7 domains, including a comprehen -
sive water management system and wastewater 
recycling system (both systems are set to be 
built around the award-winning Centennial 
Park). Furthermore, the Centralized cold room 
control system and area energy management 
system (AEMS) will enhance the efficiency of the 
overall management of both energy production 
and consumption. The use of renewable energy 
through solar roofs and solar parking will become 
a norm of this eco-urban campus and vibrant 
community. In addition, the “Creative & Startup 
Village “ is a conceptual pilot project aimed at 
creating Thailand’s first innovation district, to 
be located at the historical Block28 where the 
legacy of the Old Samyan Market stands. There 
will also be a variety of end-to-end, door-to-door, 
mobility-on-demand services, ranging from 
electric vehicle (EV) car-sharing, bike-sharing, 
Tuk-Tuk-sharing, and E-Scooter-sharing, as well 
as an EV car rental platform. Such services will 
effectively increase travel options and enhance 
the efficiency of public transport, while also 
reducing road accidents and greenhouse gas 
emissions within the area.

Province’s scenic Wang Chan District.

text:
Pain points :
Nakhon Si Thammarat Municipality
1. Some groups of people have poor health care behaviors, lack of protection against various diseases
2. The problem of slum communities lacking primary health care.
3. There are few specialized doctors at the hospital. There are not all types of medical equipment.
4. The proportion of staff providing services in the healthcare facility is less than the number of visitors to the healthcare facility.
5. The Hospital on Wheels project cannot connect to the internet signal covering all areas.

Pain points :
Suphanburi
Provincial Administrative Organization
1. There is a very clear change in the structure of age groups, the proportion of the young population (0-14 years) has decreased, while the proportion of the elderly population (60 years and over) has increased.
2. Personnel problems such as there are few civil servant personnel. Personnel are not performing their duties in the correct position, etc.
3. The work is not yet fully integrated by all parties.
4. Policy changes causing continuous work, plan new operations and performance indicators are not clear.
5. Budgets are being decrease and government subsidies are not a specified criteria.
title: Strategic Smart Living Expansion Roadmap

text:
Pain points :
Nonthaburi Provincial Administrative Organization
1. The budget of local government organizations is limited.
2. The increase in the elderly group. This will affect the living conditions and quality of life of the people in Nonthaburi Province. The proportion of elderly people (aged 60 years and over) increases every year, while the proportion of working age people and children decreases.
3. Problems with overcrowding in nursing units, such as the number of beds being insufficient for the number of patients, etc.
4. The budget is high, but the demand for the budget is also very high.

5. Self-funded and sponsored budgets are still not enough to solve the problems of the people.
6. Income collected by the Provincial Administrative Organization itself is small. Compared to income from government subsidies The revenue received from the government is uncertain.
7. Revenue collection still does not receive as much cooperation from the private sector as it should.

title: UK-Thailand Smart City Handbook

text:
This Handbook was commissioned by the UK Foreign, Commonwealth and 
Development Office (FCDO), as a complimentary research to the Prosperity 
Fund’s Global Future Cities Programme (GFCP). The Prosperity Fund is a major UK 
government initiative announced in 2015. It contributes significantly to the United 
Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and implementation of the New 
Urban Agenda. It is guided by the UK Aid Strategy objective of helping promote 
growth and prosperity in developing middle-income countries. The countries in which 
the Prosperity Fund operates offer great opportunities for creation of mutually 
beneficial partnerships, and for the growth of businesses and trade relationships. 
Background
The Global Future Cities Programme is a three year 
official development assistance programme. 
The programme will support inclusive and 
sustainable urban economic growth and increase 
global prosperity through targeted interventions on 
transportation, urban planning and urban resilience in 
ten countries worldwide (Brazil, Indonesia, Malaysia, 
Myanmar, Nigeria, Philippines, South Africa, Thailand, 
Turkey and Vietnam). 
GFCP in Thailand is being implemented in partnership 
with the Bangkok Metropolitan Administration (BMA). 
The activities will support existing objectives and 
projects in the city, while building the capacity of 
BMA teams and establishing models which can be 
replicated and up-scaled. Ongoing activities include:
Integrated Data Centre - Roadmap
Designing, and developing a road map for 
implementation, of a world-class data centre shaped 
to the specific requirements of Bangkok. The BMA 
data centre will inform and enable integrated, 
THE UK PROSPERITY FUND & GLOBAL FUTURE CITIES PROGRAMME 
sustainable, future-focused and responsive city 
planning & management.
Decision Support System for Flood Management
Optimising the impact of the BMA’s flood 
management strategy and related infrastructure 
investments, by supporting introduction of an 
effective and responsive decision-support system 
(DSS) and building the capacity of relevant teams to 
ensure that the DSS is effectively operationalised. 
Transit–Oriented Development Plan (TODP) : 
Khlong Bang Luang and Bang Wa BTS station
Assisting BMA in the preparation of a TODP for 
Khlong Bang Luang and Bang Wa BTS station, which 
has been identified as a priority development area 
by BMA and other stakeholders. This TOD will help 
demonstrate how the government’s investment in 
enhanced transport access and connectivity, can 
drive inclusive economic planning and sustainable 
development.
title: UK-Thailand Smart City Handbook

text:
Foreword
Cities are growing at an unprecedented pace. More than half of the world population 
already lives in urban areas. The proportion is forecast to increase to 66% in 
2050. Within Asia alone, this is equivalent of adding an entire new city of 3 million 
people, more than half the size of Singapore, every month for the next 30 years. 
Unless cities are well planned and managed, urbanisation exacerbates poverty, 
overcrowding, congestion, pollution. Public services quickly become inadequate 
and inaccessible. With two thirds of the world’s population due to live in cities by 
2050, the UK Government believes resolving the challenges of our cities is critical 
to resolving the issues of our future including climate change, health, resource 
depletion, poverty and waste. 
UK cities have been working for decades to tackle these new urban issues. While the 
journey is ongoing, we are keen to share our experiences and to learn from other 
countries. In Thailand, we are working in partnership with Bangkok Metropolitan 
Administration (BMA), under our Prosperity Fund’s Global Future Cities Programme, 
to support projects on establishing the city’s integrated data hub, using data to 
inform flood management policy and on Transit-Oriented Development Plan (TODP). 
Urbanisation creates many challenges, but it also presents opportunities to improve 
people’s lives. In addition to the work already underway in our Global Future Cities 
Programme, I believe there are other areas where we can work with Thailand. I hope 
this handbook will raise awareness of these opportunities and start conversations 
that lead to more collaboration between our two countries. 
Brian Davidson
HM Ambassador to Thailand
Brian Davidson
HM Ambassador to Thailand
In the Thailand 4.0 and Digital Economy Framework, ‘Smart City’ is the key umbrella 
both in promoting the innovation industry and supporting the rapidly changing 
society. In this context, the Digital Economy Promotion Agency (depa) is ‘the hub’ 
in the development of both digital manpower and technology, including standards, 
regulations and guidelines in materializing smart cities. 
Smart cities are citizen-centric. Contrary to some popular perceptions, smart cities 
do not always have to be ultra-high-tech. Instead, smart cities prefer ‘practical 
technology’ that fosters people’s wellbeing and livelihood. The key to a successful 
building of smart cities, therefore, is the understanding of the demands and needs 
of residents ‘from within.’ 
As the hub, depa connects the central and local governments, private sectors 
including startups, academic and research institutions, and external partners 
in both the ASEAN region and beyond. Currently, more than 40 cities are being 
promoted by depa and we welcome the support of our partner the British Embassy 
of Bangkok in facilitating this fruitful dialogue among our partners through this 
Smart City Handbook: Thailand.
Dr. Nattapon Nimmanpatcharin
President/CEO Digital Economy
Promotion Agency (depa)
Dr. Nattapon Nimmanpatcharin
President/CEO Digital Economy Promotion Agency (depa)
title: UK-Thailand Smart City Handbook

In particular, thank you to the Smart City Thailand team within the Digital Economy 
Promotion Agency (depa) for their time, insights, and in-kind support in developing 
this Handbook. Also, thank you to the Bangkok Metropolitan Administration’s

The project team expresses its sincere appreciation to everyone mentioned above

Urban Studies Lab (USL) was commissioned by FCDO for the research and

USL draws on its broad range of experience and expertise in the fields of urban

consulting.

text:
Department for 
International Trade (DIT) 
The Department for International Trade is the 
specialist Government department that supports: 
• Foreign companies seeking to set up or expand in 
the UK, and 
• UK-based companies to trade internationally. 
The Department for International Trade provides a 
fully integrated advisory service, delivering the latest 
business intelligence through a global network of 
commercial teams worldwide. 
The Department for International Trade works in 
close partnership with investment and economic 
development agencies in England, Scotland, Wales 
and Northern Ireland to help overseas companies to 
maximise their business objectives in the UK. 
FOR THAI COMPANIES INTERESTED IN UK 
SMART CITY PRODUCTS AND SERVICES
We can help Thai companies connect to UK companies 
through our network of trade specialists from the 
Technology & Smart Cities Team. 
This includes: 
• Identification of possible business partners 
• Information on UK Technology and Smart Cities 
• Support during visits to the UK 
FOR UK COMPANIES INTERESTED IN THE THAI 
MARKET
DIT has trade specialists who can help you commission 
services from local experts overseas. 
This includes: 
• Country and sector advice 
• Local market research 
• Support during overseas visits 
• Identification of possible business partners 
• Preparation for exhibitions and events 
FOR THAI INVESTORS INTERESTED TO INVEST IN 
THE UK 
DIT will provide you with dedicated, professional 
assistance on locating and expanding your business in 
the UK. 
DIT and our regional partners offer free, confidential 
and tailored support in a number of key areas: 
• Links with centres of excellence (e.g. universities) 
• Information on tax, regulatory and business 
planning issues 
• Information on financial incentives if applicable 
• Information on staff recruitment 
• Site and Property search assistance 
• Building key contacts- we can provide 
introductions to service providers, local, regional 
and national government and trade organisations. 
• Aftercare through on-going support 
• Maximising your global potential- Once you are 
established in the UK, we can help your company 
to take advantage of new business opportunities 
and branch out to new locations – both in the UK 
and overseas. 
For more information, please contact: 
Thailand.dit@fcdo.gov.uk
title: UK-Thailand Smart City Handbook

text:
Introduction
A Primer on 
Smart Cities
All around the world, governments and citizens are working 
towards making their cities ‘smarter’. In every one of these 
places, people are also asking the question, ‘What is a smart 
city?’ This chapter provides the basic knowledge about smart 
cities you need in order to start building cities of the future, 
especially in Thailand.
title: UK-Thailand Smart City Handbook

text:
What is a 
Smart City?
Cities have evolved over time and will 
continue to do so in perpetuity. In this 
light, the smart city should not be 
considered an end state that is easily 
attained. In fact, building the smart 
cities of the future will take time. A lot 
of time. And just as our cities are always 
evolving, so too will the ‘smart’ aspects 
of our cities need constant updates and 
attention.
If there is one simple way of 
understanding the smart city, it is that 
becoming ‘smart’ is the next logical 
step for cities to take in the context of 
emerging technologies and what we can 
do with them. 
Another important aspect of describing 
the smart city is that, in most parts of 
the world, smart city projects are very 
rarely happening at the complete city 
scale. You may be involved with the 
entire city, country or region, but more 
likely you will be working at the district, 
campus, neighbourhood, or even 
building level. 
However, these projects should be 
guided by a holistic vision of the city, 
and that is where this handbook can 
help.
Just as every city is made up of its 
own unique, constituent parts, every 
city should have its own, unique way 
of defining the smart city. So, while 
you won’t find a single definition put 
forth by this handbook, what you will 
find in this chapter are some important 
guiding principles and universal truths 
that should be considered when 
planning and developing the smart city. 
Introduction: A Primer on Smart Cities | Smart City Handbook: Thailand
8
Notice that in all three cited definitions, 
the definition contains two common 
characteristics: (1) the application of 
technology or digital innovation, and (2) 
the expected benefits they bring to the 
city.
The specific ways in which technologies 
are applied in your city, region, country 
or community will differ from any other. 
This is necessary because the people, 
organisations and goals in each city are 
unique to that time and place.
Similarly, the benefits reaped from 
applying technology will differ, and 
should be tied to the specific needs in 
your city. 
For those working to make their city 
smarter - whether you are a city leader, 
private citizen, government official, 
local entrepreneur, foreign investor, or a 
student or researcher - it’s important to 
take the time to understand your city’s 
priorities, existing assets, and, perhaps 
most importantly, your potential 
partners, in order to create your own 
definition and vision of the ‘smart city’.
DEFINITIONS OF A SMART CITY
Researchers and practitioners around 
the world that have been working on the 
smart city concept for years have come 
up with their own definitions. 
Looking at how others define ‘smart 
city’ can be useful for understanding a 
basic pattern of thinking about it, and 
can serve as a good starting point for 
others to approach their own smart city 
development projects and strategies.
“[A city] that uses information and communications 
technology (ICT) to enhance livability, workability, and 
sustainability...[done by] collecting, communicating and 
‘crunching’ [data]” 
- Smart Cities Council -
“[A city] characterised by the integration of technology 
into a strategic approach to sustainability, citizen well-
being, and economic development” 
- Navigant Research -
“[A city that] leverages digital organisational principles, 
tools and innovations to...become more sustainable, 
inclusive, successful and creative.” 
- The New Digital Deal -
title: UK-Thailand Smart City Handbook

text:
The Global Case for 
the Smart City
The motivations for cities to pursue 
their smart city vision are varied. Each 
city has its own set of issues that run 
the gamut from infrastructure to citizen 
needs that it needs to address. However, 
there are a number of powerful factors 
converging at this time that have 
compelled cities to make their cities 
smarter. 
Rapid and increasing urbanisation 
It is old news that humankind is now 
living in the urban age. More than half of 
the world’s population now live in cities, 
and driven by the economic and cultural 
opportunities our cities provide, that 
trend will only continue to grow. The 
United Nations projects that our cities 
will need to accommodate an additional 
3 billion people by 2050, and that we’ll 
need to create 40,000 new cities in the 
process. 
Growing frequency of disruptions 
Progress also has a destabilising power. 
With each step made forward, systems 
such as cities that were in harmony are 
often thrown off balance and challenged 
to readjust themselves to return to 
the centre. These disruptions come 
in all forms – societal, environmental, 
economic, and more. The COVID-19 
pandemic of 2020 is a prime example 
of a major disruption that cities need to 
prepare for and build resiliency.
Aging and inadequate infrastructure 
Cities are forever contending with 
aging infrastructure and meeting 
the demands of growing population. 
Underneath the veneer of attractive 
cities often lay crumbling and outdated 
utility systems that are not keeping 
up with developments. Municipalities 
need to deal with both the overtaxing of 
utility plants that could lead to outages 
and as well as leaky systems that are 
incurring unnecessary additional costs. 
Cities need smart city solutions to 
address these existing issues.
Citizen needs and expectations 
People want to live in places that 
make them happy, that offer them 
opportunity, and that allow them to get 
from home to work without sitting in 
traffic for two hours. They also have 
never before seen access to information 
that lets them see how other cities 
compare to their own. Simply put, 
citizens are demanding more from their 
cities and city government, and part of 
that is being smart.
Improving capabilities and decreasing 
cost of technology Fortunately, recent 
years have seen both the cost of 
smart technologies decline and their 
capabilities to make a positive impact in 
our cities improve. It has now become 
more feasible than ever before to apply 
smart solutions to address existing 
challenges, learn to anticipate future 
issues, and preempt them with a variety 
of tactics in order to provide better 
outcomes for everyone in our cities.
The global market continues to 
mature As the technologies themselves 
have become cheaper and easier to 
use, so has the number of examples of 
good practices and pool of experienced 
professionals in smart city services 
grown and expanded. While the market 
is still far from full maturity when 
compared to more traditional markets 
around the world, cities looking to enact 
their own smart city strategies and 
projects now have more support than 
ever before.
Technavio stated that the smart cities 
market – interrelated domains that 
impact urban living – was projected to 
reach $1.2 trillion USD as of 2019. This 
represents a massive opportunity for 
countries like Thailand to build smart 
cities and usher in a new marketplace 
that its citizens can partake in. This 
could stimulate innovation in its citizens. 
Once the market reaches a certain 
state of maturity, the quality of life 
for citizens and the city’s economic 
competitiveness and attractiveness will 
increase as a result.
Introduction: A Primer on Smart Cities | Smart City Handbook: Thailand 9
title: UK-Thailand Smart City Handbook

text:
If infrastructure is the circulatory system, data 
represents the red blood cells of any smart city 
travelling through it. The effective use of reliable, 
secure data from a wide range of sources and systems 
is what leads to real innovation in service provision. By 
collecting and analysing data across large numbers of 
sensors and devices, cities can gain insights that lead to 
better management of city systems, further enhancing 
competitiveness, and driving citizen-centric solutions.
In every part of the world, successful smart cities 
are the ones driven by a focus on transparency 
and open data, enabling citizens to understand and 
devise their own solutions, acquire new skills through 
online learning, and improve their interaction and 
accountability with public authorities. Cities must work 
across departments and reach out to businesses and 
citizens to develop clear expectations and rules around 
data sharing, privacy and security, allowing information 
to move seamlessly throughout the city to those that 
need it.
OPEN DATA & SECURITY
Pillars of Success 
for the Smart City
Regardless of your smart city needs and goals, 
successful smart cities must make the necessary 
investments in open and flexible ICT infrastructures. 
Smart infrastructure lays the foundations from which 
service innovation at scale is built upon, and without it, 
citywide benefits will not be possible. This starts with 
hard infrastructure, the circulatory system of high-
speed internet services, data centres and connected 
devices (sensors, measuring devices) that allow for the 
collection and communication of data. Second is the 
soft infrastructure, or the intelligent operations centres 
and cloud computing platforms needed to analyse, 
predict, and present information and deliver services. 
This is essentially the brain.
Before becoming ‘smart’, cities must advance a 
strategy for the development of an integrated smart 
infrastructure system that allows for interoperability 
and open access. What this means is that cities must 
avoid being ‘locked in’ to proprietary suppliers, and 
instead develop standards that allow any project to 
plug in, whether developed by government, business, 
or citizens. This allows cities to build projects over time 
while having confidence the pieces will all work together 
in the end.
Though every city’s journey towards 
becoming ‘smart’ is different, there 
are some ‘universal truths’ that have 
surfaced from the many smart city 
initiatives and efforts around the world. 
Entire books have been written 
describing how a city can set 
themselves up for success, regardless 
of location, culture, or size, rather than 
elaborating on every detail, this section 
distills these factors of success down 
to four essential pillars that provide 
practical guidance to anyone involved in 
making a smart city.
SMART INFRASTRUCTURE
Introduction: A Primer on Smart Cities | Smart City Handbook: Thailand10
title: UK-Thailand Smart City Handbook

text:
Though all of these other pillars are critical to the 
success of smart cities, it all starts and ends with 
people. Without a citizenry that understands how they 
may benefit from smart solutions, and that is capable of 
engaging in the process of building a smart city, there 
will never be adequate demand for the smart city to be 
realised.
Considering the degree of complexity involved in 
introducing advanced technologies to our cities, it 
can be followed that without the necessary skills and 
know-how within, not only government, but also the 
general public, it will be impossible for smart cities to 
thrive. Because of this, programmes must be put in 
place to educate and train the critical competencies of 
data sciences, IT leadership, cybersecurity, enterprise 
architecture, and others. Fortunately, the smart city 
sector has a large number of experienced global, 
regional and local consultants and service providers, 
especially in less-developed markets like Thailand, there 
is a critical opportunity to develop partnerships with 
those that have come before to seek mutually beneficial 
relationships for building skills and opening up new 
commercial markets.
SMART CITIZENS
Deploying digital transformation on any scale, in 
particular one as large as a city, requires a new way 
of thinking about urban planning, management and 
administration. Successful smart cities move to adapt 
traditional organisational models, breaking down siloes 
to focus on improved, shared outcomes for citizens. 
Technologies themselves are only as effective as the 
leaders and coordinating entities that put them to work. 
Strategic vision, cross-institutional partnerships, and 
new service delivery models are all required to drive a 
successful smart city. 
This new governance structure must include not only 
members of the city or government apparatus, but 
also an ecosystem of businesses, academia, citizens, 
local initiatives, and more. Citizen participation, public-
private partnerships, R&D projects, and other innovative 
partnership models are key to smart cities. Perhaps 
most importantly, those in charge of coordinating smart 
city efforts must have at least the basic knowledge 
of technology choices, funding options, legal and 
regulatory needs, and should be allowed to operate with 
a certain level of autonomy to work outside of existing 
governance structures and bring about change on their 
terms. 
URBAN GOVERNANCE
Introduction: A Primer on Smart Cities | Smart City Handbook: Thailand 11
title: UK-Thailand Smart City Handbook

text:
A New Marketplace of 
Opportunity
UNDERSTANDING THE SMART CITY 
ECOSYSTEM
The smart city is a new frontier for most cities. 
Even in more mature markets, supply and demand 
dynamics are still developing. Few cities have 
progressed beyond pilots or proof of concepts to 
a fully scaled and integrated smart city system. 
In cities and countries that are just beginning 
their smart city journeys, where few standards 
exist and there are very few, if any, entrenched 
market players, the path forward often feels risky 
and unknown. One thing that is clear, however, is 
the importance of leveraging partnerships and 
establishing new market mechanisms. 
The nature of smart cities requires that many 
different societal stakeholders be involved, all 
with their different and evolving roles to play. The 
following section provides a general overview of 
the typical stakeholders involved in the smart city 
ecosystem, and the groups that must be involved 
in the emerging smart city marketplace. 
For most smart city services and solutions, the citizen is the 
end user, meaning they create the demand in the smart city 
market. For smart city projects to be successful, citizens must 
be involved early and often in the process, and they can often be 
the source of innovation themselves.
With proven technologies and a large-pool of resources, large 
technology companies are an important player to support the 
scaling of solutions and to help establish industry standards.
Utilities and other providers of essential city services, like water, 
electricity, waste management and mobility, are central role 
players in leveraging smart technologies for improved service 
provision and collection and analysis of data.
Though government does not need to be the strongest player 
in the smart city market, their role as facilitator is critical to 
success. Government is best situated to articulate vision, 
provide open platforms for collaboration, set regulations, help 
create standards, and manage data. Government can often be a 
customer instead of provider for many smart city solutions.
Whereas larger companies are less likely to provide disruptive 
innovation to the market, truly novel inventions most often 
come from smaller companies, startups and even individuals. 
A successful smart city marketplace ensures these innovations 
are magnified, scaled and given market opportunity.
Schools, universities, police, hospitals, transport departments 
and other domain specific players will need specialised vendors 
that bring the necessary expertise, data know-how, and 
solutions to a particular sector.
Investors of all kinds are needed to support the new business 
ventures required for the smart city. From startups to long-term 
infrastructure, it is crucial that investment opportunities are 
made visible and matched with the right investors. 
Regulators play a critical role in the smart city, and whether a 
regulating body is fast to reform or stuck in the past can make 
or break a smart city initiative. Those involved in the smart city 
must know their regulators.
Telcos are key to providing connectivity in the system through 
their national networks of ICT infrastructures.
Around the world, higher education and research institutions, as 
well as local and international NGOs, play a key role in inventing, 
improving, measuring, benchmarking and validating the smart 
city. These entities often fill the role of mediator, bridging the 
gap between citizens and government or private stakeholders, 
and are key to a successful smart city marketplace.
GOVERNMENT 
LARGE TECHNOLOGY COMPANIES 
SMALL TECHNOLOGY COMPANIES 
TELECOMMUNICATIONS SERVICE PROVIDERS 
UTILITIES
DOMAIN-SPECIFIC VENDORS AND 
ORGANISATIONS
INVESTORS
REGULATORS
ACADEMIA & NGOs 
CITIZENS
Introduction: A Primer on Smart Cities | Smart City Handbook: Thailand12
title: UK-Thailand Smart City Handbook

text:
BUILDING THE MARKETPLACE
Smart city projects can be capital intensive 
and require specialised skillsets that 
may not be present in the market today. 
This requires governments to explore 
new funding models and partnership 
opportunities, as they cannot go it alone. 
However, this drastically oversimplifies 
what it takes to build a robust and mature 
smart city market.
Building the smart city marketplace 
requires active facilitation and support, 
and it requires moving away from 
government as the primary customer or 
provider. A mature smart city market will 
see all of the stakeholders listed on the 
previous page working together to shape 
standards, encourage demand, and nurture 
innovations to prove their value and scale 
to market. 
Most cities are not there, yet, and the 
learning curve can be steep. However, an 
important first step is to understand the 
basic process of fostering the smart city 
market. For this, we look to a simple model 
developed by Bas Boorsma in his book 
A New Digital Deal: Beyond Smart Cities. 
How to Best Leverage Digitalization for the 
Benefit of our Communities, which shows 
three interconnected methods needed for 
market creation: Create, Validate, and Scale.
Fostering a Market, Facilitating Value Creation 
The process of building a smart city market is almost never linear, but it should almost always start with 
identifying community and citizen needs, and designing service innovations based on those needs. In this 
process of value creation, a particular innovation is given the chance to mature, prove its value, be validated 
in addressing a presumed market need, and is able to scale.
VALIDATE SCALE
CREATE

and can be obtained through ‘Living Lab’ models or other pilots with real-world partners.
